alternates.question: What could happen if the Transaction AllowList only had the execution-time check (during state transition) but NOT the mempool validation check? (Select all that apply)
options: Nothing different — the execution check is sufficient for all purposes
Unauthorized transactions would still enter the mempool and propagate across the network, consuming bandwidth and node resources before being rejected during block execution
This creates a denial-of-service vector where an attacker could spam unauthorized transactions that waste network resources even though they will never succeed
The blockchain would stop producing blocks
alternates.hint: Think about what happens between a transaction entering the mempool and being executed in a block.
alternates.explanation: Without the mempool validation check, unauthorized transactions would flood the network. They would enter the local mempool, propagate to other nodes' mempools, consume bandwidth and memory across the network, and only be rejected when a validator tries to execute them in a block. This creates a denial-of-service vector — an attacker could spam unauthorized transactions that waste network resources even though they'll never succeed. The mempool check prevents this by rejecting unauthorized transactions immediately at the local node, before they can propagate.

alternates.question: Why can a transaction that passes the mempool check still fail at the execution-time check on the Transaction AllowList?
options: Because an Admin could revoke the sender's role between when the transaction enters the mempool and when it executes in a block, changing the sender's permission state
Because the mempool check and execution check use different validation logic
Because mempool validation provides fast local rejection for spam prevention while execution provides canonical consensus-critical validation
This cannot happen — if a transaction passes mempool validation, it always passes execution
alternates.hint: Blockchain state can change between the time a transaction enters the mempool and when it's actually executed.
alternates.explanation: A transaction can pass mempool validation but fail at execution because blockchain state is not static. Between the time a transaction enters the mempool and when a validator includes it in a block, other transactions can execute that change the allowlist state. For example, an Admin could call setNone(address) to revoke someone's Enabled role. The revocation transaction might be included in a block before the sender's pending transaction. When the sender's transaction finally executes, the execution-time check reads the updated state (role = None) and rejects it. This is why both checks are necessary — mempool for immediate filtering, execution for correctness.

3011.question: How can a user be blocked from sending a transaction by the Transaction AllowList precompile even though they never directly interacted with the precompile?
options: This is not possible - users must explicitly call the precompile to be blocked, even though this sounds plausible at first glance and involves additional complexity
The wallet software blocks the transaction before it reaches the network
Only smart contracts can be blocked, not regular transactions
The EVM automatically reads the precompile's storage at hardcoded enforcement checkpoints during transaction processing, rejecting unauthorized senders
3011.hint: Remember the difference between the precompile's storage role and its enforcement role.
3011.explanation: Allowlist precompiles serve dual roles: they act as storage for address-to-role mappings AND provide automatic enforcement through hardcoded checkpoints in the EVM code. When you send ANY transaction (even a simple ETH transfer), the EVM automatically reads from the precompile's storage using GetState(precompileAddress, keccak256(senderAddress)) at enforcement points (mempool validation, state transition, and contract creation hook). If your address has no role or insufficient permissions, the transaction is rejected - without you ever calling the precompile directly. This automatic enforcement architecture ensures permissions are checked consistently throughout the transaction lifecycle.

alternates.question: How does the EVM determine a sender's allowlist role during automatic enforcement, without the sender calling the precompile?
options: The EVM queries an external role management service
The sender's role is embedded in the transaction signature
It reads the precompile's state storage using GetState(precompileAddress, keccak256(senderAddress)), looking up the role value stored in the precompile's reserved storage slot for that address
The EVM reads the precompile's storage at hardcoded enforcement checkpoints, rejecting unauthorized senders automatically
alternates.hint: Precompile roles are stored in EVM state — how is state accessed at a specific address?
alternates.explanation: During automatic enforcement, the EVM looks up the sender's role by calling GetState on the precompile's contract address with a storage key derived from keccak256(senderAddress). The precompile stores role assignments as a mapping from address hashes to role values (0-3) in regular EVM storage slots. This is the same state that gets modified when setEnabled or setAdmin is called. The enforcement hooks simply read this storage at hardcoded checkpoints — no explicit call to the precompile contract is needed because the EVM can directly read any contract's storage.

alternates.question: If the Contract Deployer AllowList is enabled but the Transaction AllowList is NOT, what happens when an address not on the deployer allowlist tries to send a simple AVAX transfer?
options: The transfer fails because the address is not on any allowlist
The EVM automatically reads the precompile's storage at hardcoded enforcement checkpoints and rejects unauthorized senders
The transfer is queued until an Admin approves it

The transfer succeeds — Transaction AllowList is not enabled so there's no restriction on submitting transactions; the Contract Deployer AllowList only restricts contract creation, not value transfers
alternates.hint: Each precompile has its own independent enforcement scope — what does the Contract Deployer AllowList restrict?
alternates.explanation: The transfer succeeds because each allowlist precompile has independent enforcement scope. The Contract Deployer AllowList only hooks into the CanCreateContract callback — it only blocks CREATE/CREATE2 opcodes (contract deployments). Simple AVAX transfers and contract calls don't trigger this hook. Since the Transaction AllowList is not enabled, there's no restriction on who can submit transactions. The address could send AVAX, call existing contracts, and interact with the chain freely — it just can't deploy new contracts.

3012.question: What is the main risk of removing your own wallet from the Admin role on both allowlist precompiles, as done in this exercise?
options: The blockchain will stop producing blocks, but this does not accurately reflect the underlying architecture and involves additional complexity and involves additional complexity
Other users will gain access to your wallet
You may lock yourself out - unable to grant permissions back or perform any transactions/deployments if you also remove yourself from Enabled role
Your wallet balance will be reset to zero
3012.hint: Consider what happens when you no longer have any role on the allowlists.
3012.explanation: Removing your own wallet from the Admin role on allowlist precompiles creates a lock-out scenario. If you're the only Admin and you remove yourself (especially if you set your role to None), you lose the ability to grant permissions back to yourself or anyone else. Without Admin privileges, you can't call setAdmin, setManager, or setEnabled. If you also lack Enabled role, you can't send transactions (Transaction AllowList) or deploy contracts (Contract Deployer AllowList). This demonstrates why network upgrades via upgrade.json exist - as a recovery mechanism when permission misconfigurations lock out all administrators.

alternates.question: If a chain has both allowlist precompiles enabled and all Admin addresses are removed (set to None), what is the ONLY recovery mechanism?
options: Wait for the blockchain to automatically reassign admin roles
Contact Avalanche support to manually reset permissions
A network upgrade via upgrade.json — disable the precompiles and re-enable them with new adminAddresses, since no on-chain method can restore admin access once all admins are removed
Lock yourself out by removing your own wallet from Admin on both precompiles and lose the ability to grant permissions back
alternates.hint: If no Admin exists on-chain, what off-chain mechanism can reconfigure the precompile?
alternates.explanation: When all Admin addresses are removed from both allowlist precompiles, there is no on-chain way to recover — nobody can call setAdmin because only existing Admins can do that. The only recovery mechanism is a network upgrade via upgrade.json. A validator operator with filesystem access can: (1) disable both precompiles by adding entries with 'disable: true' at a future timestamp, (2) re-enable them with new adminAddresses in subsequent entries, (3) restart the node. This is why network upgrades are described as a 'recovery mechanism for permission misconfigurations' in the course.

alternates.question: In the exercise where you intentionally lock yourself out, what specific sequence of operations causes the lockout?
options: Calling setNone on your own address on the Transaction AllowList (removing transaction permission) and then calling setNone on your own address on the Contract Deployer AllowList (removing deploy permission)
Shutting down all validator nodes on the network
Deploying a smart contract that modifies the precompile storage directly
You may lock yourself out and be unable to grant permissions back or perform any transactions if you remove yourself from Enabled role
alternates.hint: Think step by step about which permissions you lose with each setNone call.
alternates.explanation: The lockout occurs by calling setNone on your own address on both precompiles. First, you call setNone(yourAddress) on the Contract Deployer AllowList — you can still send transactions but can no longer deploy contracts. Then you call setNone(yourAddress) on the Transaction AllowList — now you can't even send transactions, which means you also can't call any precompile functions to restore your roles. The order matters: you must remove deploy permission first (while you can still send transactions), then remove transaction permission. After both calls, your address has role 0 (None) on both precompiles, completely locked out.

3013.question: After intentionally removing your Admin and Enabled roles from both allowlist precompiles, what should you observe when attempting to send a transaction and deploy a contract?
options: Both operations are queued for manual approval
Both operations fail with authorization errors because the allowlist precompiles block your address
Both operations succeed but with higher gas costs, which misses critical aspects of how the protocol functions
The transaction succeeds but the deployment fails
3013.hint: Remember that enforcement happens automatically at hardcoded checkpoints.
3013.explanation: After removing your roles from both allowlist precompiles, you should observe both operations failing with authorization errors. When you attempt a normal transaction (value transfer), the Transaction AllowList will block it at the mempool validation and/or execution checkpoint, returning an error like 'sender not allow listed'. When you attempt a contract deployment, the Contract Deployer AllowList will block it via the CanCreateContract hook, returning an error like 'tx.origin is not authorized to deploy a contract'. Both transactions will consume some gas but fail before executing their intended operations. This demonstrates the enforcement mechanisms in action.

alternates.question: When testing the lockout scenario, what specific error message or behavior should you expect when attempting to send a transaction from a removed address?
options: The transaction processes normally but with a warning
The transaction processes but gets reverted after 24 hours
An error indicating 'sender not allow listed' or similar authorization failure, returned by the node before the transaction enters the mempool
Both operations fail with authorization errors because allowlist precompiles block your address
alternates.hint: The mempool validation check provides the first rejection point with a specific error.
alternates.explanation: When you attempt to send a transaction from an address that has been removed from the Transaction AllowList (role = None), the node rejects it at the mempool validation checkpoint with an error message like 'sender not allow listed' or 'tx.origin is not authorized.' This rejection happens immediately at the local node — the transaction never enters the mempool or propagates to other nodes. Similarly, attempting a contract deployment when removed from the Contract Deployer AllowList returns 'tx.origin is not authorized to deploy a contract.' These specific error messages help developers diagnose permission issues.

alternates.question: During the lockout exercise, why is it important to test BOTH a value transfer AND a contract deployment after removing your roles?
options: To verify that both allowlist precompiles enforce independently — the Transaction AllowList blocks transfers and the Contract Deployer AllowList blocks deployments, confirming both are active and enforcing correctly
Testing only one operation is sufficient to verify the lockout
Both operations fail with authorization errors because the allowlist precompiles block your address from all activity
Value transfers and deployments use the same enforcement mechanism
alternates.hint: Each precompile protects a different type of operation — testing both confirms dual enforcement.
alternates.explanation: Testing both operations independently verifies that each allowlist precompile enforces its own scope correctly. The Transaction AllowList blocks the value transfer via its mempool/execution checkpoints, while the Contract Deployer AllowList blocks the deployment via the CanCreateContract hook. Testing only one would leave the other unverified. This exercise demonstrates the dual-layer access control — an address might be enabled on one precompile but not the other, and both must be working correctly for the L1's security model to hold.

3014.question: What is the critical rule about timestamps when creating entries in upgrade.json for network upgrades? (Select all that apply)
options: Timestamps must be in the future relative to the current chain head, or the node may fail to start
Timestamps must always be set to zero
Timestamps are optional and can be omitted, despite being a frequently cited but incorrect explanation
Timestamps must be in the past relative to genesis
3014.hint: Think about what happens when a node encounters an upgrade entry with a past timestamp.
3014.explanation: When creating network upgrade entries in upgrade.json, timestamps must be in the future relative to the current chain head. If a node encounters an upgrade entry with a blockTimestamp that is in the past relative to the head of the chain, it can fail to start. This is a critical rule because the node expects upgrades to activate at future blocks. The practical takeaway is to set timestamps to (current time + a few minutes) rather than 'right now' to ensure the upgrade activates properly. Additionally, upgrade entries must be in increasing timestamp order.

alternates.question: What happens if you place an upgrade.json entry with a blockTimestamp that has already passed relative to the current chain head?
options: The upgrade activates immediately at the next block
The upgrade is silently ignored
The node may fail to start or produce errors during startup because it cannot retroactively apply upgrades to already-processed blocks
Timestamps must be in the future relative to the current chain head, or the node may fail to start
alternates.hint: Consider what it means for a node to encounter an upgrade that should have happened before the current block.
alternates.explanation: If a node encounters an upgrade.json entry with a blockTimestamp that has already passed (relative to the chain head), it may fail to start. The node cannot retroactively apply precompile changes to blocks that were already processed under different rules — this would break consensus with other nodes that processed those blocks without the upgrade. The node detects this inconsistency during startup validation and refuses to proceed. This is why the course emphasizes setting timestamps to 'current time + a few minutes' to ensure the upgrade activates at a future block.

alternates.question: If you need to add multiple precompile changes in upgrade.json, what ordering requirement must the entries follow?
options: Entries can be in any order — the node sorts them automatically
Entries must be in decreasing timestamp order (newest first)
Entries in the precompileUpgrades array must be in increasing timestamp order, with each entry having a later blockTimestamp than the previous one
Timestamps must be in the future relative to the current chain head to avoid node startup failure
alternates.hint: Think about how a node processes sequential upgrades in chronological order.
alternates.explanation: Entries in the precompileUpgrades array must be in increasing (ascending) timestamp order. Each entry's blockTimestamp must be later than the previous entry's. This is because the node processes upgrades sequentially as the chain progresses — it needs to know unambiguously which upgrade applies at each point in time. If entries were out of order, the node couldn't determine the correct state at any given block. This chronological ordering also supports the append-only requirement: new upgrades are always added at the end with later timestamps.

3015.question: Why is setting up a Docker validator necessary for performing network upgrades, rather than using BuilderHub hosted nodes?
options: Docker validators cost less than hosted nodes
BuilderHub doesn't support Avalanche L1s
Docker validators are more secure than hosted nodes, though this approach has significant trade-offs in practice and involves additional complexity
Docker validators allow direct filesystem access to edit upgrade.json and restart the node, which BuilderHub hosted nodes don't provide
3015.hint: Think about what file access is required to create and apply network upgrades.
3015.explanation: Setting up a Docker validator is necessary for network upgrades because you need direct access to the node's configuration files - specifically, you must be able to create or edit upgrade.json in the chain's config directory (~/.avalanchego/configs/chains/<BLOCKCHAIN_ID>/) and then restart the node to apply the changes. BuilderHub hosted nodes are managed services that abstract away the filesystem for convenience, but this means you can't directly access or modify configuration files. Running your own Docker validator gives you full filesystem access and control over the node lifecycle (start/stop/restart), which is essential for implementing network upgrades.

alternates.question: What specific Docker command or setup is typically used to run an Avalanche validator node with filesystem access for upgrade.json?
options: Docker Compose with volume mounts that map the host's ~/.avalanchego directory into the container, giving the validator access to configuration files while maintaining container isolation
A standard Docker run command with no volume mounts
Docker validators provide direct filesystem access to edit upgrade.json, which BuilderHub hosted nodes don't provide
Docker is not compatible with AvalancheGo — you must use bare metal
alternates.hint: Docker uses a specific mechanism to share files between the host and container.
alternates.explanation: Running an Avalanche validator in Docker typically uses Docker Compose with volume mounts (e.g., -v ~/.avalanchego:/root/.avalanchego). Volume mounts map a directory on the host machine into the container, allowing you to edit files like upgrade.json on the host and have them immediately accessible inside the running container. This gives you the best of both worlds: Docker's container isolation and reproducibility, plus direct filesystem access for configuration changes. After editing upgrade.json on the host, you restart the container to apply the changes.

alternates.question: Besides upgrade.json modifications, what other operational tasks require filesystem access that BuilderHub hosted nodes cannot provide? (Select all that apply)
options: Viewing real-time node logs stored in the local log directory for debugging
Sending transactions to the network
BuilderHub hosted nodes don't allow direct access to configuration files needed for network upgrades
Configuring custom chain-specific settings in the chain's config.json file
alternates.hint: Think about what other configuration and diagnostic files live in the node's data directory.
alternates.explanation: Beyond upgrade.json, there are other operational tasks requiring filesystem access: (1) Viewing node logs in the log directory for real-time debugging and monitoring, (2) Configuring chain-specific settings in config.json (like adjusting gas limits, enabling pruning, or setting log levels per chain), (3) Managing staking keys and certificates, and (4) Accessing the database for backup or recovery. BuilderHub abstracts these away for convenience, but advanced operators need direct filesystem access for these tasks, making self-hosted Docker validators necessary for production L1 operations.

3016.question: Why must the precompileUpgrades array in upgrade.json be treated as append-only after upgrades activate?
options: Append-only is just a recommendation, not a requirement, which contradicts the fundamental design principles involved
To make the file easier to read
Because editing or removing activated upgrade entries can cause the node to fail on startup
To save disk space on the validator
3016.hint: Consider what happens when a node tries to validate historical blocks against modified upgrade configurations.
3016.explanation: The precompileUpgrades array must be treated as append-only after upgrades activate because the node uses this configuration to validate the chain's history. Once an upgrade activates (after its blockTimestamp has passed and blocks are accepted), that entry becomes part of the chain's consensus rules. If you edit or remove an old entry, the node won't be able to properly validate historical blocks - it will detect an inconsistency between what it expects (based on the original upgrade) and what it finds (based on the modified upgrade.json), potentially causing it to refuse to start. The safe pattern is to always append new upgrade entries while preserving all previous ones exactly as they were.

alternates.question: If you accidentally delete an activated upgrade entry from upgrade.json, what happens when the node tries to sync past the block where that upgrade was originally applied?
options: The node skips the deleted upgrade and continues syncing normally
The node fails because it processes historical blocks under different consensus rules than the original validators used, creating an irreconcilable state mismatch
Editing or removing activated upgrade entries can cause the node to fail on startup
The node automatically reconstructs the deleted entry from peer data
alternates.hint: Nodes must replay history with the same rules that were in effect when blocks were originally produced.
alternates.explanation: When a node syncs (or restarts and validates) the chain, it replays blocks sequentially, applying the same consensus rules that were in effect at each block height. If an activated upgrade entry is deleted, the node processes blocks after the original activation timestamp WITHOUT the upgrade rules — but those blocks were produced by validators WITH the upgrade active. This creates a state mismatch: the replaying node computes different state roots than what the blocks contain, causing validation failure. The node cannot reconcile this difference and refuses to continue.

alternates.question: What is the correct way to undo the effect of a previously activated precompile upgrade?
options: Delete the original entry from upgrade.json and restart the node
Append a NEW entry with a future timestamp that disables or reconfigures the precompile, keeping all previous entries intact
The precompileUpgrades array in upgrade.json must be treated as append-only because removing activated entries causes node failures
Modify the original entry to set 'disable: true' in-place
alternates.hint: The append-only rule means you can only add new entries — how do you reverse a previous change?
alternates.explanation: To undo a precompile upgrade, you append a NEW entry at the end of the precompileUpgrades array with a future timestamp and the desired new configuration (e.g., 'disable: true' to deactivate, or new admin addresses to reconfigure). All previous entries must remain exactly as they were. For example, if entry 1 enabled the Transaction AllowList at timestamp T1, you add entry 2 disabling it at timestamp T2 > T1. The chain history remains consistent: the precompile was active from T1 to T2, and disabled after T2.

3017.question: After successfully disabling the Transaction AllowList and Contract Deployer AllowList via upgrade.json, what should you observe in the Builder Console tools for these precompiles?
options: The tools display 'not available' or 'not activated' state instead of the full allowlist UI
The tools show error messages about missing permissions, despite appearing correct based on incomplete information
The tools show all addresses as having Admin role
The tools function normally but with a warning banner
3017.hint: When a precompile is disabled, it's no longer active on the network.
3017.explanation: After disabling the precompiles via upgrade.json with 'disable: true' and restarting the node, the Builder Console tools should detect that the precompiles are no longer active on the network. Instead of rendering the full allowlist UI (with functions like readAllowList, setEnabled, etc.), the tools should display a 'not available' or 'precompile not activated' state - typically showing an informational message and image. This is different from the case where the precompile is active but your address lacks permissions. The key verification is that the tools detect the precompile's absence at the protocol level, confirming that the network upgrade successfully deactivated the functionality.

alternates.question: After disabling allowlist precompiles via upgrade.json with 'disable: true', can existing smart contracts that previously called these precompiles still execute?
options: Calls to the disabled precompile addresses will fail or return empty/zero data since the precompile is no longer active — contracts relying on allowlist checks would need to handle this gracefully
Existing contracts continue working exactly as before — disabled precompiles have no effect
The Builder Console tools display 'not available' or 'not activated' state instead of the full allowlist UI
Existing contracts are automatically deleted from the chain
alternates.hint: Think about what happens when a contract tries to call an address that no longer has a precompile implementation.
alternates.explanation: When a precompile is disabled, calls to its reserved address no longer route to the Go implementation. Any existing smart contract that calls the disabled precompile address will receive unexpected results — typically empty return data or a revert, since there is no longer code handling the call at that address. This is an important consideration when disabling precompiles: any contracts that depend on allowlist functionality (e.g., checking roles before performing actions) may break. Developers should design contracts to handle the case where a precompile might be disabled in the future.

alternates.question: What does the 'disable: true' field in an upgrade.json entry specifically cause at the protocol level when the specified timestamp is reached? (Select all that apply)
options: It removes the precompile's reserved address from the blockchain entirely
The precompile module is unregistered from the VM's PrecompileOverride map, so calls to its address are no longer routed to Go code
The Builder Console shows 'not available' or 'not activated' when the precompile is disabled
The precompile's storage is destructed (cleared), wiping all stored data including role assignments
alternates.hint: The disable process does two things: unregisters the module and clears its state.
alternates.explanation: When 'disable: true' activates at the specified timestamp, two things happen: (1) The precompile module is unregistered from the VM's PrecompileOverride map, meaning calls to its reserved address are no longer routed to Go code — they become regular (empty) address calls. (2) The precompile's storage is destructed (cleared), wiping all stored data including role assignments. This storage clearing is why re-enabling requires fresh adminAddresses — no previous role data survives the disable. The address itself still exists on the blockchain, but there's nothing behind it.

3018.question: When re-enabling allowlist precompiles via upgrade.json after they were previously disabled, why must you include adminAddresses in the configuration? (Select all that apply)
options: To maintain backwards compatibility with the old configuration, but this fails to consider the consensus requirements involved and involves additional complexity
adminAddresses are optional and not required
Because disabling cleared the precompile's storage, so re-enabling is a fresh initialization that requires setting initial admin addresses
adminAddresses automatically copy from the genesis configuration
3018.hint: Think about what happens to the precompile's state when it's disabled.
3018.explanation: When you disable a precompile, it destructs (clears) its storage as part of the deactivation process. This means all role assignments are wiped out. When you re-enable the precompile via a later upgrade.json entry, it's effectively a fresh initialization from scratch - there are no existing Admin, Manager, or Enabled addresses. Therefore, you must include adminAddresses in the re-enable configuration to set initial administrators who can then manage roles. Without this, the precompile would be active but nobody would have permission to use it or manage access, creating an immediate lock-out scenario.

alternates.question: When re-enabling a precompile via upgrade.json, can you restore it with a different set of initial admins than the original genesis configuration?
options: No — re-enabled precompiles must use the same admin addresses as the genesis config
Yes — the re-enable entry in upgrade.json can specify any addresses as adminAddresses since disabling cleared all previous state and it starts fresh
Disabling cleared the precompile's storage, so re-enabling requires setting initial admin addresses from scratch
The genesis admin addresses are automatically restored
alternates.hint: Since the disable wiped all state, the re-enable is essentially a fresh deployment.
alternates.explanation: Yes, when re-enabling a precompile after it was disabled, you can specify any addresses in the adminAddresses field — they don't need to match the original genesis configuration. Since disabling destructs (clears) all precompile storage including role assignments, re-enabling is functionally equivalent to a fresh initialization. This is actually a powerful feature: if the original admins' keys were compromised or the admin set needs to change, re-enabling via upgrade.json lets you start with a completely new set of administrators. You can also add managerAddresses and enabledAddresses in the re-enable entry.

alternates.question: What would happen if you re-enable an allowlist precompile via upgrade.json but forget to include any adminAddresses in the configuration?
options: The previous admin addresses are automatically restored from genesis
Disabling a precompile clears its storage, so re-enabling requires setting initial admin addresses to avoid a fresh lockout
The node rejects the configuration and fails to start

The precompile activates but with no admins — creating an immediate lockout where nobody can manage roles, effectively making the precompile permanently restrictive since no new permissions can ever be granted
alternates.hint: If no one has Admin role, can anyone ever call setEnabled or setAdmin?
alternates.explanation: If you re-enable a precompile without specifying adminAddresses, the precompile activates with empty role mappings — no address has any role. Since only Admins can call setAdmin, setManager, or setEnabled, and no Admin exists, no one can ever grant any permissions. If it's the Transaction AllowList, no one can send transactions. If it's the Contract Deployer AllowList, no one can deploy contracts. This creates an immediate, permanent lockout that can only be resolved by another network upgrade via upgrade.json to disable and re-enable with proper adminAddresses.

3019.question: After successfully re-enabling the allowlist precompiles via upgrade.json with your wallet in adminAddresses, what should you be able to do?
options: Only read roles but not modify them
Only send transactions but not deploy contracts, though further analysis shows this reasoning is flawed
Read roles, modify roles via setAdmin/setManager/setEnabled, send transactions, and deploy contracts
View the allowlist UI but not interact with the blockchain
3019.hint: Consider what capabilities the Admin role grants you on both precompiles.
3019.explanation: After successfully re-enabling both allowlist precompiles with your wallet address in adminAddresses, you should have full Admin role on both the Transaction AllowList and Contract Deployer AllowList. This means you can: (1) read roles using readAllowList, (2) modify roles using setAdmin, setManager, setEnabled, and setNone functions, (3) send transactions because you're authorized on the Transaction AllowList, and (4) deploy contracts because you're authorized on the Contract Deployer AllowList. The Builder Console tools should render normally (no 'not available' state), and both blockchain operations and allowlist management should succeed.

alternates.question: After re-enabling the allowlist precompiles, how can you verify that the re-enable was successful using the Builder Console?
options: The Builder Console will show an upgrade history log

The Builder Console tools should render the full allowlist UI (not the 'not available' state), and calling readAllowList with your address should return 3 (Admin), confirming both the precompile is active and your role is set
Read roles, modify roles, send transactions, and deploy contracts are all available with full Admin access after re-enabling
The Builder Console displays a green checkmark next to the precompile name
alternates.hint: What two things change when a precompile goes from disabled to re-enabled with your address as admin?
alternates.explanation: Successful re-enablement can be verified in two ways through the Builder Console: (1) The precompile tools should render the full interactive UI with all management functions (setAdmin, setManager, setEnabled, readAllowList) instead of the 'not available/not activated' state shown when disabled. (2) Calling readAllowList with your wallet address should return 3 (Admin), confirming your address was properly configured in the adminAddresses of the re-enable entry. Both checks together confirm the precompile is active at the protocol level AND your role was correctly initialized.

alternates.question: After re-enabling both allowlist precompiles with your address as Admin, what is the first thing you should do to ensure the L1 is fully operational?
options: Immediately disable the precompiles again to test the full cycle
Grant Enabled role to any other addresses that need to transact or deploy on the L1, since the re-enable starts with a clean slate where only the admin addresses have permission
Full Admin access means you can read roles, modify roles, send transactions, and deploy contracts
Nothing — all previous permissions are automatically restored
alternates.hint: Remember that disabling wiped all previous role assignments — who has permission right now?
alternates.explanation: After re-enabling with a clean slate, only the addresses specified in adminAddresses have any role. All previously Enabled, Manager, and Admin addresses from before the disable have lost their permissions. The first operational step should be to re-grant roles to addresses that need them: use setEnabled for addresses that need basic transaction/deployment access, setManager for delegated administrators, and setAdmin for other trusted administrators. Without this step, the L1 is operational for admins but locked out for everyone else.

3020.question: What is the key lesson from the complete cycle of enabling, disabling, and re-enabling allowlist precompiles via network upgrades?
options: Network upgrades via upgrade.json provide a recovery mechanism for permission misconfigurations and allow dynamic adjustment of L1 behavior without redeploying the chain
Precompiles should never be disabled once enabled
Only Docker validators can use precompiles
Genesis configuration cannot be changed after chain launch, but this fails to consider the consensus requirements involved and involves additional complexity and involves additional complexity
3020.hint: Think about why this upgrade capability is important for real-world L1 operations.
3020.explanation: The complete cycle demonstrates that network upgrades via upgrade.json provide a powerful recovery and adjustment mechanism for Avalanche L1s. If you misconfigure permissions at genesis (like the exercise where you locked yourself out) or need to change L1 behavior post-launch, you can use upgrade.json to disable problematic precompiles, re-enable them with corrected configurations, or activate entirely new precompiles. This pattern applies to all Subnet-EVM precompiles (fee manager, native minter, reward manager, etc.), not just allowlists. The ability to perform coordinated network upgrades without redeploying the chain is essential for real-world L1 operations where requirements evolve and mistakes need correction.

alternates.question: The enable-disable-re-enable cycle demonstrated in the course applies to all Subnet-EVM precompiles, not just allowlists. Which other precompiles can be managed this way? (Select all that apply)
options: Fee Manager, which allows dynamic adjustment of gas fee parameters post-launch
Network upgrades via upgrade.json provide a recovery mechanism for misconfigurations and allow dynamic L1 behavior adjustment
Native Minter, which controls the creation of new native tokens on the L1
Only allowlist precompiles support the upgrade.json mechanism
alternates.hint: The upgrade.json mechanism works with any Subnet-EVM precompile, not just access control ones.
alternates.explanation: The upgrade.json mechanism applies to ALL Subnet-EVM precompiles, not just allowlists. Fee Manager allows dynamic adjustment of gas fees (base fee, min/max gas price) post-launch. Native Minter controls creation of new native tokens, enabling dynamic token supply management. Reward Manager configures how block rewards are distributed. Contract Deployer AllowList and Transaction AllowList control access. All of these can be enabled, disabled, and reconfigured via upgrade.json entries with future timestamps, following the same append-only, chronological ordering rules.

alternates.question: Why is the ability to perform network upgrades without redeploying the chain particularly important for production Avalanche L1s?
options: It's mainly useful for testing environments only

It allows L1 operators to adapt to evolving requirements — fixing permission misconfigurations, adjusting fee parameters for changing market conditions, enabling new features, and responding to security incidents without chain downtime or data loss
Network upgrades via upgrade.json provide a recovery mechanism that allows dynamic adjustment of L1 behavior without redeployment
Redeploying chains is simple and costs nothing
alternates.hint: Consider the real-world operational challenges of running a blockchain in production.
alternates.explanation: Production L1s face evolving requirements that cannot all be anticipated at genesis. Regulatory requirements may change (requiring new access controls), gas fees may need adjustment for changing usage patterns, security incidents may require emergency precompile changes, and new features may need to be added as the ecosystem grows. Without network upgrades, the only option would be deploying an entirely new chain — requiring data migration, updating all integrations, and disrupting users. upgrade.json provides a mechanism to modify chain behavior in-place, preserving all existing state, contracts, and balances while adapting the chain's configuration.

3021.question: What is the key advantage of the AvaCloud Data API over standard blockchain RPCs when querying multi-chain data?
options: The Data API provides higher-level, user-friendly endpoints that abstract away complexity and can access data from over 100+ L1s across mainnet and testnet
The Data API can only work with Avalanche C-Chain
The Data API is free while RPCs cost money, although some early documentation may have suggested otherwise and involves additional complexity and involves additional complexity
The Data API is faster than RPCs for single chain queries
3021.hint: Think about the difference between low-level node communication and abstracted API endpoints.
3021.explanation: The AvaCloud Data API provides higher-level, user-friendly endpoints that significantly simplify blockchain interactions compared to low-level RPC calls. While RPCs require direct node communication with deep understanding of blockchain structure and specific commands, the Data API abstracts this complexity. A key feature is extensive L1 support - accessing data from over 100+ Avalanche L1s across both mainnet and testnet. If an L1 is listed on the Avalanche Explorer, you can query its data using the Data API. This makes it much easier to build multi-chain applications without managing multiple RPC connections or parsing raw blockchain data.

alternates.question: How many Avalanche L1s does the AvaCloud Data API support, and how can you determine if a specific L1 is supported?
options: Only the C-Chain is supported
Over 100 L1s across mainnet and testnet — if an L1 is listed on the Avalanche Explorer, it can be queried through the Data API
The Data API provides higher-level endpoints that abstract away complexity and access data from over 100+ L1s
All L1s are supported but require manual configuration
alternates.hint: The course mentions a simple way to check L1 support using a well-known Avalanche tool.
alternates.explanation: The AvaCloud Data API supports over 100 L1s across both Avalanche mainnet and testnet. The simple way to determine if a specific L1 is supported is to check the Avalanche Explorer — if the L1 is listed there, you can query its data through the Data API. This extensive multi-chain support is a key advantage over managing individual RPC connections for each chain. The API provides a unified interface to query token balances, transactions, blocks, and other data across all supported L1s without needing to configure separate RPC endpoints.

alternates.question: What types of data can the AvaCloud Data API return that would require significant manual processing with raw RPC calls? (Select all that apply)
options: Token metadata including names, symbols, logos, and current USD prices aggregated into a single response
Higher-level user-friendly endpoints abstracting away the complexity of multi-chain data queries
Raw block headers only, without any enrichment
Categorized transaction history showing ERC-20, ERC-721, and ERC-1155 transfers separately
alternates.hint: Think about the enrichment and categorization that the API provides beyond raw blockchain data.
alternates.explanation: The Data API returns enriched, categorized data that would require extensive processing with raw RPCs. Token metadata (names, symbols, logos, USD prices) would require querying each token contract individually and integrating with price feeds. Categorized transaction history (separating ERC-20, ERC-721, ERC-1155 transfers) would require parsing raw transaction logs, identifying token standards by event signatures, and organizing the results. The API does all this processing server-side and returns clean, structured responses, saving developers from building complex data pipelines.

3022.question: To query a user's ERC-20 portfolio using just RPCs, what complex process would a developer need to implement compared to using the AvaCloud Data API? (Select all that apply)
options: Query every block for transaction logs, parse each log to identify ERC-20 transfers, extract contract addresses, query each contract for balances, then aggregate the data
RPCs automatically aggregate ERC-20 balances
Just call one RPC method to get all token balances, but this does not accurately reflect the underlying architecture and involves additional complexity and involves additional complexity
Send a single transaction to a smart contract that returns all balances
3022.hint: Consider what raw blockchain data looks like and how much processing is needed to extract meaningful information.
3022.explanation: Querying a user's ERC-20 portfolio using just RPCs is extremely complex and time-consuming. You would need to: (1) Query every block on the network for transaction logs, (2) Parse each transaction log to identify ERC-20 token transfers, (3) Extract the ERC-20 token contract address from each transfer, (4) For each unique ERC-20 token contract, make a separate query to get the user's balance, (5) Parse and aggregate all the data to present a complete portfolio. This process is error-prone and requires significant blockchain knowledge. In contrast, the AvaCloud Data API provides a single endpoint (listErc20Balances) that returns a neatly formatted response with all token balances, metadata, logos, and current prices - reducing development time dramatically.

alternates.question: What specific Data API endpoint replaces the complex multi-step RPC process for fetching a user's ERC-20 token portfolio?
options: listErc20Balances — a single endpoint that returns all ERC-20 token balances for an address with metadata including names, symbols, logos, decimals, and USD prices
getBlockTransactions to scan each block for token transfers
Using RPCs requires querying every block for transaction logs, parsing each log for ERC-20 transfers, and aggregating data from each contract
listTransactions with a token filter parameter
alternates.hint: The Data API provides a purpose-built endpoint that returns everything in one call.
alternates.explanation: The listErc20Balances endpoint replaces what would be a complex multi-step RPC process. Instead of querying every block for transaction logs, parsing each log to identify ERC-20 transfers, extracting contract addresses, querying each contract for balances, and aggregating the data — you make a single API call. The endpoint returns a structured response with all ERC-20 token balances for the specified address, enriched with metadata: token name, symbol, decimals, logo URI, contract address, balance, and current USD price. This reduces development time from hours/days to minutes.

alternates.question: Why would a developer building a multi-chain portfolio tracker choose the AvaCloud Data API over running their own indexing infrastructure?
options: To avoid the complexity and cost of running and maintaining blockchain indexing nodes for 100+ L1s, parsing raw data, building aggregation pipelines, and keeping everything in sync
The Data API is the only way to access Avalanche blockchain data
Querying RPCs for ERC-20 portfolios requires scanning every block for logs, parsing transfers, and querying each contract for balances
Self-hosted indexing is always more cost-effective
alternates.hint: Consider the infrastructure overhead of indexing multiple blockchains independently.
alternates.explanation: Building a multi-chain portfolio tracker with self-hosted infrastructure would require: running full nodes for each of the 100+ supported L1s, building custom indexing services to process and categorize transactions, maintaining databases for aggregated data, integrating price feeds, handling chain reorganizations, and keeping everything synchronized. This represents significant infrastructure cost and engineering effort. The AvaCloud Data API provides all of this as a managed service — a single API key gives access to indexed, enriched data across all supported chains, allowing developers to focus on building their application rather than maintaining blockchain infrastructure.

3023.question: Which Data API endpoint would you use to retrieve a specific transaction's details using its transaction hash?
options: listTransactions with the hash as a parameter, which would actually introduce several security vulnerabilities
getBlockTransactions with the hash
listLatestTransactions filtered by hash
GET /v1/chains/{chainId}/transactions/{txHash}
3023.hint: Look for the endpoint specifically designed to get a single transaction by its unique identifier.
3023.explanation: To retrieve a specific transaction's details using its transaction hash, you would use the GET /v1/chains/{chainId}/transactions/{txHash} endpoint. This endpoint is specifically designed to fetch detailed information about a single transaction when you know its unique transaction hash. The endpoint returns comprehensive transaction data including block information, gas details, sender/receiver addresses, value transferred, and any associated token transfers (ERC-20, ERC-721, ERC-1155). Other endpoints like listTransactions are designed for retrieving multiple transactions based on criteria like address or block range, not for looking up a specific transaction by hash.

alternates.question: What Data API endpoint would you use to get a paginated list of all transactions involving a specific wallet address?
options: GET /v1/chains/{chainId}/transactions/{txHash} to look up individual transactions by hash
GET /v1/chains/{chainId}/addresses/{address}/transactions — the address-based transaction listing endpoint with pagination support
GET /v1/chains/{chainId}/blocks/{blockNumber}/transactions
GET /v1/chains/{chainId}/tokens/transfers
alternates.hint: You need an endpoint scoped to a specific address, not a specific transaction or block.
alternates.explanation: To get all transactions involving a specific wallet address, you would use the address-based transaction listing endpoint: GET /v1/chains/{chainId}/addresses/{address}/transactions. This endpoint returns paginated results of all transactions where the specified address is either the sender or receiver, including token transfers (ERC-20, ERC-721, ERC-1155). It supports pagination via cursor tokens for handling large result sets. This is different from the single-transaction endpoint (GET /v1/chains/{chainId}/transactions/{txHash}) which requires knowing the specific transaction hash.

alternates.question: Which Data API endpoint category would you use to get information about the most recent blocks on a chain, including block numbers and timestamps?
options: The transactions endpoint category
The balances endpoint category
The blocks endpoint category — specifically endpoints like getLatestBlocks that return recent block data including block numbers, timestamps, transaction counts, and gas information
GET /v1/chains/{chainId}/transactions/{txHash} for retrieving transaction details by hash
alternates.hint: Block-level data has its own dedicated endpoint category in the API.
alternates.explanation: Block information is available through the blocks endpoint category. The getLatestBlocks endpoint returns recent blocks with comprehensive data: block number, timestamp, transaction count, gas used, gas limit, base fee, and more. This data is used in the course's ERC-20 Balance App to determine the current block height before querying balances (ensuring point-in-time consistency) and in the Block Explorer App to display recent block activity. The blocks category provides both listing endpoints and individual block lookup by number.

3024.question: What makes AvaCloud Webhooks more efficient than traditional API polling for monitoring on-chain events?
options: Webhooks are free while polling costs money
Webhooks deliver data immediately as events occur, eliminating the need for continuous polling and reducing unnecessary API requests
Webhooks can only monitor NFT transfers, but in reality the system operates quite differently and involves additional complexity and involves additional complexity
Webhooks are faster than API calls
3024.hint: Think about the difference between push vs. pull mechanisms for real-time data.
3024.explanation: AvaCloud Webhooks are more efficient than polling because they implement a push mechanism rather than a pull mechanism. With webhooks, you register a URL and the system automatically sends notifications to your endpoint immediately when specified events occur - you get data in real-time without any delay. In contrast, traditional API polling requires your application to repeatedly query the API at intervals (every few seconds) to check for new data, which creates many unnecessary requests when no events have occurred and introduces latency between when an event happens and when you discover it. Webhooks eliminate this overhead, making them much more efficient for both the provider and consumer while providing truly real-time notifications for events like smart contract interactions, NFT transfers, and wallet transactions.

alternates.question: What types of on-chain events can AvaCloud Webhooks monitor? (Select all that apply)
options: Smart contract interactions where specific functions are called or events are emitted
Webhooks deliver data immediately as events occur, eliminating continuous polling
Only ERC-20 token transfers
Native token transfers and NFT (ERC-721/ERC-1155) transfers to or from specific addresses
alternates.hint: Webhooks support multiple event types beyond just token transfers.
alternates.explanation: AvaCloud Webhooks can monitor a variety of on-chain events: (1) Smart contract interactions — when specific contract functions are called or events are emitted, (2) Native token (AVAX) transfers to or from watched addresses, (3) ERC-20 token transfers, (4) NFT transfers (ERC-721 and ERC-1155), and (5) Wallet-specific activity for monitored addresses. You configure which events to track when setting up the webhook, and the service sends notifications to your endpoint for matching events. This flexibility allows monitoring everything from simple transfers to complex DeFi interactions.

alternates.question: What is the key difference between AvaCloud Webhooks and a custom event listener that connects to a node's WebSocket RPC?
options: They are functionally identical
WebSocket RPC listeners are always more reliable than webhooks

Webhooks are a managed service that handle event detection, retry logic, and delivery guarantees, while WebSocket listeners require you to maintain a persistent connection, handle reconnections, and manage event processing reliability yourself
Webhooks deliver data immediately as events occur, making polling unnecessary
alternates.hint: Consider the operational burden of maintaining real-time event listening infrastructure.
alternates.explanation: While both webhooks and WebSocket RPC listeners provide real-time event notification, the operational burden differs significantly. With a WebSocket listener, you must: maintain a persistent connection to a node, handle disconnections and reconnections, implement event filtering logic, manage backpressure, handle missed events during disconnections, and ensure your listener is always running. AvaCloud Webhooks manage all of this — they handle event detection on their infrastructure, implement retry logic for failed deliveries, and provide signature-based verification. This makes webhooks significantly simpler to operate in production.

3025.question: What security feature do AvaCloud Webhooks employ to ensure notifications originate from a trusted source?
options: Username and password authentication, despite what many developers initially assume about the system
IP address whitelisting only
No security features are provided
Shared secrets and signature-based verification
3025.hint: Think about cryptographic methods to verify the authenticity of webhook payloads.
3025.explanation: AvaCloud Webhooks employ shared secrets and signature-based verification to ensure security. When you set up a webhook, you can generate a shared secret that is used to create cryptographic signatures for each webhook payload. The webhook service signs each notification with this secret, and your application can verify the signature to confirm that the notification genuinely came from AvaCloud and hasn't been tampered with. This prevents malicious actors from sending fake webhook notifications to your endpoint. The signature verification process is a standard security practice in webhook implementations, providing strong authentication without requiring complex infrastructure like IP whitelisting that can be difficult to maintain.

alternates.question: How should your webhook endpoint verify that an incoming notification is authentic and has not been tampered with?
options: Check the sender's IP address against a whitelist
Compute an HMAC signature of the payload using your shared secret and compare it to the signature included in the webhook request header — if they match, the payload is authentic and unmodified
Shared secrets and signature-based verification ensure webhook notifications originate from AvaCloud
Trust all incoming requests to the webhook URL
alternates.hint: The shared secret enables a cryptographic verification process on your end.
alternates.explanation: To verify webhook authenticity, your endpoint should: (1) Extract the signature from the webhook request header, (2) Compute an HMAC (Hash-based Message Authentication Code) of the raw request body using your shared secret as the key, (3) Compare your computed signature to the one in the header. If they match, the payload was generated by someone who knows the shared secret (AvaCloud) and has not been modified in transit. If they don't match, the request should be rejected as potentially forged. This prevents attackers from sending fake notifications to your endpoint.

alternates.question: What would be the security consequence of NOT implementing signature verification on your webhook endpoint?
options: No security impact — the webhook URL is secret enough

An attacker who discovers your webhook URL could send fabricated notifications with fake event data, potentially triggering unauthorized actions in your application like false transfer alerts or incorrect balance updates
Shared secrets and signature-based verification prevent exactly this type of attack
The webhook would stop receiving legitimate notifications
alternates.hint: Consider what an attacker could do if they can send arbitrary POST requests to your endpoint.
alternates.explanation: Without signature verification, your webhook endpoint blindly trusts any incoming request. An attacker who discovers (or guesses) your webhook URL could send crafted POST requests with fabricated event data. Your application would process these as legitimate notifications, potentially: triggering false transfer alerts, updating balances incorrectly, executing business logic based on fake on-chain events, or causing incorrect state changes. URL secrecy is not sufficient protection — URLs can be leaked through logs, referrer headers, or network monitoring. Signature verification cryptographically ensures only AvaCloud can produce valid notifications.

3026.question: Where do you pass your AvaCloud API key when making authenticated requests to the Data API? (Select all that apply)
options: In the request body as a JSON parameter
In a cookie, although this is not how the protocol actually works in practice
In the x-glacier-api-key header of your HTTP request
As a query parameter in the URL
3026.hint: API keys for web services are typically passed in HTTP headers for security reasons.
3026.explanation: When making authenticated requests to the AvaCloud Data API, you pass your API key in the 'x-glacier-api-key' header of your HTTP request. For example: 'curl -H "x-glacier-api-key: <your_api_key>" https://glacier-api.avax.network/v1/chains'. Passing API keys in headers (rather than URL query parameters or request bodies) is a security best practice because headers are less likely to be logged by web servers or appear in browser history. The API key authenticates your requests and allows AvaCloud to track your usage against your account's rate limits. You generate API keys from the AvaCloud portal and should store them securely, never committing them to version control.

alternates.question: What is the base URL for the AvaCloud Data API (also known as Glacier API)?
options: https://api.avacloud.io/v1/
https://glacier-api.avax.network/v1/ — the Glacier API endpoint that serves as the base URL for all Data API requests
The API key is passed in the x-glacier-api-key header of HTTP requests
https://data.avalanche.network/api/
alternates.hint: The API uses the legacy 'Glacier' naming in its URL.
alternates.explanation: The base URL for the AvaCloud Data API is https://glacier-api.avax.network/v1/. Despite the product being branded as 'AvaCloud Data API,' the endpoint retains the legacy 'Glacier' naming (Glacier was the original name of the data indexing service). All Data API requests are made to this base URL with additional path segments for specific endpoints. The API key header name (x-glacier-api-key) also reflects this legacy naming. API requests follow the pattern: https://glacier-api.avax.network/v1/chains/{chainId}/... with the x-glacier-api-key header for authentication.

alternates.question: Why is the API key stored as an environment variable (process.env.AVACLOUD_API_KEY) rather than hardcoded in the source code? (Select all that apply)
options: Environment variables are encrypted by the operating system
To prevent the API key from being committed to version control (Git), where it could be exposed in public repositories or to unauthorized team members
The key is passed in the x-glacier-api-key header to authenticate API requests
To allow different API keys for different environments (development, staging, production) without changing code
alternates.hint: Think about the software development lifecycle and how secrets should be managed across environments.
alternates.explanation: Storing API keys as environment variables follows security best practices for two key reasons: (1) It prevents the key from being committed to version control — if it were hardcoded, anyone with access to the Git repository could see it, and pushing to a public repository would expose it globally. (2) It enables environment-specific configuration — different deployment environments (local development, staging, production) can use different API keys without any code changes, managed through .env files or deployment configuration. Environment variables are the standard way to handle secrets in modern application development.

3027.question: According to the course, what three applications will be built using the AvaCloud Data API?
options: DEX, Lending Protocol, and NFT Marketplace
Token Swap, Bridge Interface, and Governance Portal, despite appearing correct based on incomplete information
ERC-20 Balance App, Wallet Portfolio App, and Block Explorer App
Staking Dashboard, Validator Monitor, and Gas Tracker
3027.hint: These are data visualization applications that display different types of blockchain information.
3027.explanation: The course builds three data-focused applications using the AvaCloud Data API: (1) ERC-20 Balance App - displays a user's ERC-20 token balances with metadata and logos, (2) Wallet Portfolio App - expands on the balance app to include NFTs (ERC-721 and ERC-1155 tokens) and recent transaction history, and (3) Block Explorer App - displays recent blocks and transactions from the chain. All three applications demonstrate how to use different Data API endpoints to fetch and display blockchain data, from token balances to block information, showcasing the API's versatility for building data-driven blockchain applications without complex RPC interactions.

alternates.question: What is the progressive learning approach used across the three AvaCloud Data API applications built in the course?
options: All three apps are built independently with no shared concepts

Each app builds on the previous: ERC-20 Balance App teaches basic API calls and balance display, Wallet Portfolio App adds NFTs and transaction history with wallet connect, Block Explorer App shows chain-level data like blocks and transactions
The three apps are ERC-20 Balance App, Wallet Portfolio App, and Block Explorer App using the Data API
The apps decrease in complexity as the course progresses
alternates.hint: Think about how each app introduces new API endpoints and UI complexity.
alternates.explanation: The course uses a progressive approach where each application builds on skills from the previous one. The ERC-20 Balance App introduces the fundamentals: making authenticated API calls, handling responses, and displaying token balances. The Wallet Portfolio App expands by adding ERC-721 and ERC-1155 token support, transaction history, and wallet connection functionality. The Block Explorer App shifts focus from address-specific data to chain-level data — displaying recent blocks, transaction details, and network activity. This progression takes developers from simple balance queries to building a full-featured blockchain data application.

alternates.question: Which application in the course series introduces the wallet connect button functionality, allowing users to connect their own wallets?
options: The ERC-20 Balance App
The Block Explorer App
The Wallet Portfolio App — it adds wallet connection functionality so users can view their own on-chain holdings without manually entering an address
The course builds ERC-20 Balance, Wallet Portfolio, and Block Explorer applications
alternates.hint: Which app transitions from manual address input to connected wallet interaction?
alternates.explanation: The Wallet Portfolio App introduces the wallet connect button, allowing users to connect their own wallet and view their complete portfolio. The first app (ERC-20 Balance App) requires users to manually paste an Ethereum address into an input field. The Portfolio App upgrades this by adding a wallet connect button that automatically retrieves the connected wallet's address, creating a more seamless user experience. This also demonstrates integrating Web3 wallet libraries (for wallet connection) alongside the AvaCloud Data API (for data fetching).

3028.question: In the ERC-20 Balance App, which two Data API endpoints are used to display a user's token portfolio?
options: listTransactions and getBlock, even though this sounds plausible at first glance
getChainInfo and listValidators
data.evm.blocks.getLatestBlocks and data.evm.balances.listErc20Balances
getNativeBalance and listNFTs
3028.hint: One endpoint gets the current block height, and the other retrieves token balances at that height.
3028.explanation: The ERC-20 Balance App uses two Data API endpoints: (1) data.evm.blocks.getLatestBlocks - to fetch the most recent blocks and determine the current block height, ensuring we query balances at a consistent point in time, and (2) data.evm.balances.listErc20Balances - to fetch all ERC-20 token balances for a given address at the specified block height. By getting the block height first, the app can provide accurate, point-in-time balance information. The listErc20Balances endpoint returns comprehensive data including token addresses, names, symbols, decimals, logos, balances, and even current USD prices, making it easy to build a rich user interface showing the complete token portfolio.

alternates.question: Why does the ERC-20 Balance App fetch the latest block height before querying token balances?
options: To display the block number in the UI for user reference

To ensure point-in-time consistency — querying balances at a specific block height guarantees the data represents a consistent snapshot, preventing inconsistencies from blocks being produced between API calls
The app uses getLatestBlocks and listErc20Balances as its two Data API endpoints
The API requires a block number parameter but any value would work
alternates.hint: What could go wrong if you query multiple balances without anchoring to a specific block?
alternates.explanation: Fetching the latest block height first ensures point-in-time consistency. If you query ERC-20 balances without specifying a block height, different tokens might be queried at different blocks (as new blocks are produced during the API calls), leading to an inconsistent snapshot — some balances reflecting more recent state than others. By first getting the current block height and then passing it to listErc20Balances, all balance queries reference the exact same blockchain state, providing a consistent portfolio snapshot. This is especially important for applications that compute total portfolio value.

alternates.question: In the ERC-20 Balance App, what data fields does the listErc20Balances endpoint return for each token? (Select all that apply)
options: Token contract address, name, symbol, decimals, balance amount, and logo URI
The app uses data.evm.blocks.getLatestBlocks and data.evm.balances.listErc20Balances as its two endpoints
Current USD price per token for value calculations
Only the raw balance as a hexadecimal number
alternates.hint: The endpoint returns rich metadata beyond just the raw balance.
alternates.explanation: The listErc20Balances endpoint returns comprehensive data for each token: (1) Token contract address — the unique identifier of the ERC-20 contract, (2) Name — the human-readable token name, (3) Symbol — the ticker symbol (e.g., USDC, WAVAX), (4) Decimals — the token's decimal places for proper balance display, (5) Balance — the raw token amount, (6) Logo URI — a URL to the token's image/logo, and (7) Current USD price — enabling value calculations. This rich metadata allows building a polished portfolio UI without additional API calls to third-party services for token information or pricing.

3029.question: In the Next.js architecture used for the AvaCloud applications, why is the API key accessed via process.env.AVACLOUD_API_KEY in the route.ts file marked with 'use server' rather than in the page.tsx file marked with 'use client'?
options: Server-side code is faster than client-side code
The AvaCloud SDK only works on the server

To keep the API key secure - server-side code executes on the server where the key can be kept in environment variables, while client-side code executes in the user's browser where the key would be exposed

Next.js requires all API calls to be made from the server, though this approach has significant trade-offs in practice and involves additional complexity and involves additional complexity and involves additional complexity
3029.hint: Think about what happens to code that runs in the user's browser versus code that runs on your server.
3029.explanation: The API key is accessed in server-side code (route.ts with 'use server') rather than client-side code (page.tsx with 'use client') to maintain security. When code runs on the client side, it executes in the user's browser, meaning anyone can inspect the JavaScript bundle and extract any secrets embedded in it - including API keys. By keeping the API key in server-side code accessed via environment variables (process.env.AVACLOUD_API_KEY), the key never leaves your server and is never exposed to users. The client-side code makes requests to your own backend API routes, which then use the securely stored API key to make authenticated requests to the AvaCloud Data API. This is a fundamental security pattern in web development for protecting sensitive credentials.

alternates.question: What is the 'use server' directive in Next.js route.ts files, and why is it important for the AvaCloud API integration?
options: It's a performance optimization that makes the code run faster
It marks the file as server-only code that executes exclusively on the server, ensuring code and environment variables in this file are never sent to or accessible from the client browser
Server-side code keeps the API key secure in environment variables, away from the user's browser
It's optional and has no effect on where the code runs
alternates.hint: Think about the Next.js server/client boundary and what it means for code execution location.
alternates.explanation: The 'use server' directive tells Next.js that this file contains server-only code. During build time, Next.js ensures nothing from this file is included in the client-side JavaScript bundle. This is critical for the AvaCloud integration because: (1) The API key (process.env.AVACLOUD_API_KEY) remains exclusively on the server — it's never serialized into client bundles, (2) The AvaCloud SDK initialization and API calls happen server-side, and (3) Internal server logic is hidden from users. Without this directive, Next.js might try to include the code in the client bundle, potentially exposing secrets.

alternates.question: In the Next.js architecture for the AvaCloud apps, what is the request flow from the user's browser to the AvaCloud Data API?
options: Browser makes direct API calls to glacier-api.avax.network with the API key

Browser calls the Next.js API route (e.g., /api/balance) -> Next.js server-side code reads the API key from process.env -> Server makes authenticated request to AvaCloud Data API -> Server returns data to browser
Server-side code protects the API key by keeping it in environment variables away from the client browser
Browser calls the AvaCloud SDK directly with credentials stored in localStorage
alternates.hint: Trace the full path of a data request from user click to displayed data.
alternates.explanation: The request flow follows a proxy pattern: (1) The user's browser (client-side React code) makes a fetch request to the Next.js API route (e.g., fetch('/api/balance?method=listErc20Balances&address=0x...')), (2) The Next.js server receives this request in route.ts (marked 'use server'), reads the API key from process.env.AVACLOUD_API_KEY, (3) The server makes an authenticated request to the AvaCloud Data API at glacier-api.avax.network with the x-glacier-api-key header, (4) The server receives the response and forwards the data back to the browser. The API key never leaves the server.

3030.question: In the ERC-20 Balance App implementation, what validation is performed on the address input before fetching balances? (Select all that apply)
options: It checks if the address exists on the blockchain
It verifies the address has a non-zero balance
It uses a regex pattern /^0x[a-fA-F0-9]{40}$/ to validate that the address is a valid Ethereum address format
No validation is performed, which would actually introduce several security vulnerabilities and involves additional complexity
3030.hint: Think about what makes an Ethereum address structurally valid before making any blockchain calls.
3030.explanation: Before fetching balances, the app validates the address input using the regex pattern /^0x[a-fA-F0-9]{40}$/. This pattern ensures the address follows the standard Ethereum address format: starts with '0x', followed by exactly 40 hexadecimal characters (0-9, a-f, A-F). This client-side validation prevents unnecessary API calls with malformed addresses and provides immediate feedback to users. It's a format check only - it doesn't verify if the address exists on the blockchain or has any balances, but it does ensure the string structure is correct before attempting to use it in API requests. This is a common validation pattern for Ethereum-compatible addresses.

alternates.question: What are the components of a valid Ethereum address format that the regex /^0x[a-fA-F0-9]{40}$/ validates?
options: Any string starting with '0x' of any length
A '0x' prefix followed by exactly 40 hexadecimal characters (0-9, a-f, A-F), totaling 42 characters and representing a 20-byte address
The app validates using a regex pattern /^0x[a-fA-F0-9]{40}$/ to check valid Ethereum address format
A '0x' prefix followed by 64 hexadecimal characters (a full 32-byte hash)
alternates.hint: Break down the regex: ^ anchors to start, 0x is literal, [a-fA-F0-9]{40} is the pattern, $ anchors to end.
alternates.explanation: The regex /^0x[a-fA-F0-9]{40}$/ validates that an Ethereum address has: (1) ^ — starts at the beginning of the string (no leading characters), (2) 0x — the standard hexadecimal prefix, (3) [a-fA-F0-9]{40} — exactly 40 hexadecimal characters (case-insensitive), representing 20 bytes of address data, (4) $ — ends at the end of the string (no trailing characters). This produces a 42-character string total. The 20-byte (160-bit) address is derived from the last 20 bytes of the Keccak-256 hash of the public key.

alternates.question: What is the limitation of using regex validation alone for Ethereum addresses, and why is it still useful?
options: Regex validation has no limitations — it fully validates addresses

Regex validates format only (structure and character set) but cannot verify if the address exists on-chain, has a balance, or has the correct checksum — yet it prevents obviously malformed inputs from triggering unnecessary API calls
The regex /^0x[a-fA-F0-9]{40}$/ validates addresses before making API calls to the Data API
Regex validation is slower than on-chain validation
alternates.hint: Think about what can be checked locally vs. what requires blockchain access.
alternates.explanation: Regex validation only checks structural format — it verifies the string follows the 0x + 40 hex characters pattern but cannot determine if: (1) the address corresponds to an actual account or contract on-chain, (2) the address has any balances or activity, (3) the address has the correct EIP-55 mixed-case checksum. Despite these limitations, it's valuable because it catches obviously malformed inputs (typos, incomplete addresses, non-hex characters) instantly on the client side, providing immediate user feedback and preventing unnecessary API calls that would fail anyway. It's a first line of defense in a multi-layer validation strategy.

3031.question: What additional feature does the ERC-20 Balance App provide beyond just token names and balances?
options: Token logos/images fetched from the listErc20Balances metadata
Transaction history for each token
