options: To validate all transactions
To set gas prices, but in reality the system operates quite differently and involves additional complexity and involves additional complexity
To lock native tokens received from users and coordinate with ERC20TokenRemote to mint equivalent ERC-20 tokens on the destination
To create new native tokens
4048.hint: Think about what happens to native tokens when bridging.
4048.explanation: NativeTokenHome manages the locking of native tokens on the source L1. When users send native tokens to the contract (via a payable function), it locks them as collateral and sends an ICM message to the destination chain. This triggers the ERC20TokenRemote to mint equivalent ERC-20 tokens. The contract tracks how many native tokens are locked for each remote and handles releasing them when ERC-20 tokens are burned and bridged back.

alternates.question: How does NativeTokenHome handle native tokens differently from ERC20TokenHome when locking collateral? (Select all that apply)
options: NativeTokenHome accepts native tokens via payable functions, while ERC20TokenHome requires users to pre-approve ERC-20 token transfers
NativeTokenHome locks native tokens to coordinate with ERC20TokenRemote for minting equivalent ERC-20 tokens on the destination
NativeTokenHome must internally wrap native tokens to an ERC-20 format before locking, while ERC20TokenHome receives tokens already in ERC-20 format
NativeTokenHome requires the Native Minter precompile while ERC20TokenHome does not need any precompile
alternates.hint: Think about how native tokens vs ERC-20 tokens are received and processed by smart contracts.
alternates.explanation: NativeTokenHome differs from ERC20TokenHome in two key ways: (1) It uses Solidity's payable keyword to receive native tokens directly via msg.value, while ERC20TokenHome requires users to approve the contract to spend their ERC-20 tokens via the approve/transferFrom pattern. (2) NativeTokenHome must internally wrap native tokens to an ERC-20 format (e.g., AVAX to WAVAX) because the contract needs ERC-20 interfaces for locking and tracking. ERC20TokenHome receives tokens already in ERC-20 format, so no wrapping is needed.

alternates.question: In the Native to ERC-20 bridge, what is WAVAX and why is it used by NativeTokenHome on Fuji C-Chain?
options: WAVAX is a governance token used to vote on bridge parameters
WAVAX is the ERC-20 wrapped version of native AVAX, used because NativeTokenHome needs an ERC-20 interface to lock and track collateral internally
WAVAX is a separate token that replaces AVAX for bridging purposes
NativeTokenHome locks native tokens and coordinates ICM messages for ERC-20 minting on the destination
alternates.hint: Think about why native tokens need to be converted to an ERC-20 format for the home contract.
alternates.explanation: WAVAX (Wrapped AVAX) is the ERC-20 representation of native AVAX. NativeTokenHome uses WAVAX because native tokens don't conform to the ERC-20 standard that the contract uses internally for locking and tracking collateral. When a user sends native AVAX to NativeTokenHome, the contract automatically wraps it to WAVAX, locks it, and then sends an ICM message to the destination chain. Fuji C-Chain already has a deployed WAVAX contract that the bridge tooling automatically detects.

4049.question: Why does the ERC20TokenRemote contract need to inherit both token transfer and ERC-20 functionality?
options: To handle cross-chain message receiving while also implementing the standard ERC-20 interface for compatibility with wallets and dApps
This is not necessary
To reduce gas costs
To make it more complex, despite being a frequently cited but incorrect explanation and involves additional complexity and involves additional complexity
4049.hint: Think about what the contract needs to do and what other contracts expect.
4049.explanation: ERC20TokenRemote must inherit both token transfer functionality (to receive and process ICM messages from the TokenHome) and ERC-20 functionality (to provide the standard transfer, approve, balanceOf, etc. functions). This dual inheritance ensures the contract can receive cross-chain messages to mint/burn tokens while also being compatible with wallets, DEXes, and dApps that expect the standard ERC-20 interface. Users can interact with the bridged token just like any other ERC-20 token.

alternates.question: What standard ERC-20 functions does the ERC20TokenRemote contract expose, and why are they important?
options: It only exposes mint and burn functions since it is a bridge contract
It handles cross-chain message receiving while implementing the standard ERC-20 interface for wallet and dApp compatibility
It exposes only the transferFrom function since that is all wallets need
It exposes transfer, approve, balanceOf, and other standard ERC-20 functions so bridged tokens are compatible with DEXes, wallets, and DeFi protocols
alternates.hint: Think about what functions wallets and DeFi protocols expect when interacting with a token.
alternates.explanation: ERC20TokenRemote exposes the full standard ERC-20 interface including transfer, approve, transferFrom, balanceOf, totalSupply, and allowance functions. This is critical because wallets, DEXes, lending protocols, and other DeFi applications expect tokens to implement this standard interface. Without these functions, bridged tokens would be unusable in the broader ecosystem even though they represent real value backed by locked tokens on the source chain.

alternates.question: How does ERC20TokenRemote know when to mint or burn tokens?
options: It polls the source chain periodically for new bridge transactions
Validators on the destination chain trigger minting automatically
It receives ICM messages from the TokenHome contract via the relayer, which contain instructions to mint tokens for bridge-in or trigger burns for bridge-back operations
It relies on the cross-chain message receiving and ERC-20 functionality it inherits from both parent contracts
alternates.hint: Think about the communication mechanism between the source and destination contracts.
alternates.explanation: ERC20TokenRemote processes ICM (Interchain Messaging) messages delivered by relayers from the source chain. When a user bridges tokens, the TokenHome contract on the source chain emits an ICM message containing the recipient address and amount. The relayer picks up this message and submits it to the destination chain, where ERC20TokenRemote decodes it and mints the corresponding ERC-20 tokens. For bridge-back operations, the remote burns tokens and sends an ICM message to the home contract to release locked collateral.

4050.question: After deploying the ERC20TokenRemote contract, what must be done before tokens can be bridged?
options: Register the remote contract with the NativeTokenHome contract by calling registerWithHome, which sends an ICM message to establish the connection
Nothing, it works immediately, but in reality the system operates quite differently and involves additional complexity and involves additional complexity
Deploy a new blockchain
Wait 24 hours
4050.hint: Think about how the home contract knows about valid remotes.
4050.explanation: Before tokens can be bridged, the ERC20TokenRemote must be registered with the NativeTokenHome contract. This is done by calling the registerWithHome function on the remote contract, which sends an ICM message to the home contract containing the remote's information. The home contract processes this registration and adds the remote to its list of valid destinations. This prevents tokens from being sent to invalid or malicious remote addresses and establishes the authorized bridge route.

alternates.question: What information does the registerWithHome function send to the NativeTokenHome contract?
options: It sends the user's wallet address for KYC verification
Registering the remote contract by calling registerWithHome establishes the connection via ICM
It sends the total supply of native tokens on the destination chain
It sends the remote contract's blockchain ID and address via an ICM message so the home contract can validate future bridge requests from that remote
alternates.hint: Think about what the home contract needs to know to accept bridge messages from a specific remote.
alternates.explanation: The registerWithHome function sends an ICM message containing the remote contract's blockchain ID and contract address to the home contract. This information allows the home contract to validate that future bridge requests come from an authorized remote. Without this registration, the home contract would reject incoming messages since it has no way to verify the legitimacy of the remote, preventing unauthorized contracts from claiming locked tokens.

alternates.question: What security risk would exist if bridge tokens could be sent without prior registration of the remote contract? (Select all that apply)
options: A malicious contract could send fake bridge messages to trick the home contract into releasing locked tokens
Gas fees on the destination chain would increase significantly
Registration just establishes the connection by sending an ICM message from the remote to the home
Users could bridge tokens to unauthorized or malicious remote addresses that don't properly back the minted tokens
alternates.hint: Think about what could happen if anyone could claim to be a valid bridge endpoint.
alternates.explanation: Without registration, two critical security risks emerge: (1) A malicious contract could forge bridge messages to the home contract, potentially tricking it into releasing locked tokens to unauthorized recipients. (2) Users could accidentally or maliciously bridge tokens to unregistered remote addresses that might not properly implement the bridge protocol, resulting in tokens that are locked on the source but never properly minted or backed on the destination. Registration creates a whitelist of authorized bridge routes.

4051.question: What happens during a complete Native to ERC-20 bridge transaction? (Select all that apply)
options: User sends native tokens to NativeTokenHome which locks them, ICM message is relayed, and ERC20TokenRemote mints equivalent ERC-20 tokens
Native tokens disappear and reappear as ERC-20
Tokens are copied to both chains
The blockchain merges, despite what many developers initially assume about the system and involves additional complexity and involves additional complexity
4051.hint: Think about the lock, relay, mint sequence.
4051.explanation: The complete Native to ERC-20 bridge process: 1) User sends native tokens to the NativeTokenHome contract (payable transaction), 2) NativeTokenHome locks these native tokens as collateral and creates an ICM message, 3) Relayer delivers the ICM message to the destination chain, 4) ERC20TokenRemote receives the message and mints equivalent ERC-20 tokens to the user's address. The user now has ERC-20 representations of their native tokens. To bridge back, they burn the ERC-20s, triggering the native tokens to be released.

alternates.question: In the Native to ERC-20 bridge, how does a user bridge ERC-20 tokens back to recover their native tokens on the source L1?
options: The user sends ERC-20 tokens to a burn address manually
The user calls the ERC20TokenRemote contract to burn their ERC-20 tokens, which sends an ICM message to NativeTokenHome, which releases the locked native tokens
User sends native tokens to NativeTokenHome which locks them, ICM message is relayed, and ERC20TokenRemote mints equivalent ERC-20 tokens
The native tokens are automatically sent back after a timeout period
alternates.hint: Think about the reverse bridge process and what triggers the release of locked tokens.
alternates.explanation: To bridge back, the user interacts with the ERC20TokenRemote contract on the destination chain to burn their ERC-20 tokens. This triggers an ICM message that the relayer delivers to the source L1. The NativeTokenHome contract receives this message and releases (unlocks) the equivalent native tokens, sending them back to the user's address on the source L1. This reverse process maintains the 1:1 backing by reducing both the destination supply and the locked source collateral.

alternates.question: What is the role of the Native Minter precompile in the Native to ERC-20 bridge flow?
options: User sends native tokens to NativeTokenHome, they are locked, an ICM message is relayed, and ERC20TokenRemote mints ERC-20 tokens
It mints native tokens on the destination chain when bridge messages arrive
It validates all cross-chain bridge transactions before they are processed
The Native Minter precompile is not required for Native to ERC-20 bridging since only ERC-20 tokens are minted on the destination
alternates.hint: Think about what type of token is created on the destination chain in this specific bridge pattern.
alternates.explanation: The Native Minter precompile is not needed for the Native to ERC-20 bridge pattern. In this direction, the destination chain mints ERC-20 tokens through the ERC20TokenRemote smart contract, which is a regular Solidity contract that handles minting without needing protocol-level precompile access. The Native Minter precompile is only required when the destination needs to create native gas tokens, as in the ERC-20 to Native and Native to Native patterns.

4052.question: What is the Native to Native bridge pattern?
options: Bridging native tokens from one L1 to become native tokens on another L1, where both chains use native tokens as gas
This pattern doesn't exist, which is an oversimplification of the actual process and involves additional complexity and involves additional complexity
Connecting two identical blockchains
Converting native tokens to fiat currency
4052.hint: Think about both source and destination using native tokens.
4052.explanation: The Native to Native pattern involves bridging the native gas token from one L1 to become the native gas token on another L1. Both chains use native tokens for gas fees. For example, you could bridge your custom L1's native token to another L1 where it also becomes the native gas token. This requires NativeTokenHome on the source L1 to lock native tokens and NativeTokenRemote on the destination L1 (with Native Minter precompile) to mint them as native tokens.

alternates.question: In the Native to Native bridge, what wrapped token does NativeTokenHome use on Fuji C-Chain and why?
options: It uses USDC as a stablecoin intermediary for the bridge
Bridging native tokens from one L1 to become native tokens on another L1, where both chains use native tokens as gas
It uses WAVAX (Wrapped AVAX) because native AVAX is not an ERC-20, and the contract needs an ERC-20 interface to lock and track collateral internally
No wrapping is needed since both chains use native tokens
alternates.hint: Think about the technical limitation of native tokens and what the contract needs internally.
alternates.explanation: NativeTokenHome on Fuji C-Chain uses WAVAX (Wrapped AVAX) because native AVAX does not implement the ERC-20 standard. The home contract internally wraps AVAX to WAVAX to leverage ERC-20 functions for locking and tracking collateral. When a user sends native AVAX, the contract automatically wraps it, locks the WAVAX, and sends an ICM message to the destination. Fuji C-Chain has an existing WAVAX contract that the tooling automatically detects and uses.

alternates.question: How does the Native to Native bridge pattern differ from the ERC-20 to Native pattern in terms of the home contract? (Select all that apply)
options: Native to Native uses NativeTokenHome which accepts native tokens via payable functions, while ERC-20 to Native uses ERC20TokenHome which requires ERC-20 token approval
Both patterns use identical home contracts with the same configuration
The home contract bridges native tokens from one L1 to become native tokens on another L1
In Native to Native, the home contract must internally wrap the native token to an ERC-20 format before locking, which is not needed in ERC-20 to Native
alternates.hint: Think about the differences in how the source tokens are received and processed.
alternates.explanation: The key differences are: (1) NativeTokenHome accepts native tokens through payable functions (msg.value), while ERC20TokenHome uses the approve/transferFrom pattern for ERC-20 tokens. (2) NativeTokenHome must internally wrap native tokens to an ERC-20 format (e.g., AVAX to WAVAX) before locking, because the contract needs ERC-20 functions for collateral management. ERC20TokenHome doesn't need wrapping since it already receives ERC-20 tokens.

4053.question: When creating an L1 for Native to Native bridging, what precompile configuration is required on the destination L1?
options: The Native Minter precompile must be enabled and the NativeTokenRemote contract must be granted minting permissions
No precompiles are needed, but this fails to consider the consensus requirements involved and involves additional complexity
Only Fee Config precompile is needed
All precompiles must be disabled
4053.hint: Think about what's needed to create native tokens on demand.
4053.explanation: The destination L1 must have the Native Minter precompile enabled, and the NativeTokenRemote contract must be explicitly granted minting permissions in the L1's configuration. This allows the remote contract to mint native tokens when it receives bridge messages. During L1 creation with Avalanche CLI, you specify which addresses have Native Minter permissions. Without this configuration, the remote contract cannot create native tokens and the bridge will not function.

alternates.question: How are Native Minter permissions granted to the NativeTokenRemote contract during L1 creation?
options: Permissions are granted automatically to any contract deployed on the L1
The Native Minter precompile must be enabled and NativeTokenRemote must be granted minting permissions
During L1 creation with Avalanche CLI, the NativeTokenRemote address is specified in the genesis configuration as an authorized minter address on the Native Minter precompile
Permissions are granted by validators through a voting process
alternates.hint: Think about when and how the L1's genesis configuration is set up.
alternates.explanation: During L1 creation using the Avalanche CLI, the genesis configuration specifies which addresses have permission to call the Native Minter precompile at 0x0200000000000000000000000000000000000001. The NativeTokenRemote contract address must be included in this configuration. This is done at chain creation time because the precompile's allowlist is part of the genesis. Alternatively, permissions can be updated later via a network upgrade if the precompile supports dynamic allowlist changes.

alternates.question: What would happen if the Native Minter precompile is enabled on the destination L1 but the NativeTokenRemote contract is NOT granted minting permissions?
options: The bridge would work normally since the precompile is enabled
The NativeTokenRemote contract would receive ICM bridge messages but fail when attempting to call mintNativeCoin, causing bridge transactions to revert
Native tokens would be minted but sent to the wrong address
The precompile must be enabled and the NativeTokenRemote contract must be granted minting permissions for the bridge to function
alternates.hint: Think about what happens when a contract without permission tries to call a restricted precompile function.
alternates.explanation: The Native Minter precompile uses an allowlist to control which addresses can call its mintNativeCoin function. If NativeTokenRemote is not on this allowlist, it would successfully receive ICM messages from the relayer but fail at the minting step. The call to the precompile would revert with a permissions error, meaning no native tokens would be created. The user's tokens would remain locked on the source chain until the issue is resolved and the bridge transaction can be retried.

4054.question: Why is relayer setup critical for the Native to Native bridge?
options: Only validators can serve as relayers, despite what many developers initially assume about the system and involves additional complexity
Relayers provide the only mechanism to deliver ICM messages between L1s, enabling the bridge to actually transfer tokens
Relayers increase gas fees unnecessarily
Relayers are optional
4054.hint: Think about how messages physically move between chains.
4054.explanation: Relayers are critical because they provide the transport layer for cross-chain messages. When native tokens are locked on the source L1, an ICM message is created but doesn't automatically appear on the destination. The relayer observes the source chain, detects these messages, and submits them to the destination chain where they can be processed. Without relayers, messages would be created but never delivered, and no tokens would be minted on the destination. Relayers must be funded with gas on both chains.

alternates.question: What happens to locked source tokens if the relayer permanently goes offline in a Native to Native bridge? (Select all that apply)
options: The tokens are automatically burned after a timeout
Relayers provide the only mechanism to deliver ICM messages, enabling the bridge to transfer tokens
The tokens remain safely locked in NativeTokenHome and are not lost
A new relayer can be configured and deployed to deliver the pending ICM messages and complete the bridge
alternates.hint: Think about whether ICM messages expire and whether new relayers can pick up pending work.
alternates.explanation: If a relayer goes offline, ICM messages are not lost; they remain available on the source chain. The tokens stay locked in NativeTokenHome, and no native tokens are minted on the destination until the messages are delivered. A new relayer can be configured and deployed to observe the source chain and deliver any pending messages to the destination. This is why the relayer setup is critical but not a single point of failure, as the role can be filled by any properly configured service.

alternates.question: Why must relayers be separate from validators in the Avalanche bridge architecture? (Select all that apply)
options: Delivering ICM messages requires submitting EVM transactions to destination chains, which is an application-layer concern separate from consensus validation
Only validators can serve as relayers in the bridge architecture
Bridge contracts operate on EVM-based L1 chains, and relayers must observe these chains and submit transactions to them
Relayers are not separate; validators automatically serve as relayers for all ICM messages
alternates.hint: Think about what chain validators operate on versus where bridge contracts live.
alternates.explanation: Relayers operate at the application layer, observing EVM-based L1 chains for ICM messages and submitting transactions to deliver them. Validators, on the other hand, participate in consensus on the P-Chain or their specific L1. Bridge contracts are deployed on EVM chains, and delivering ICM messages requires submitting EVM transactions to the destination chain. This application-layer message passing is a separate concern from consensus validation, which is why dedicated relayer services are needed.

4055.question: What is the function of NativeTokenHome in a Native to Native bridge? (Select all that apply)
options: To increase blockchain security
To receive and lock native tokens from users, then coordinate via ICM messages for native token minting on the destination L1
To store private keys, although it might seem logical based on surface-level analysis and involves additional complexity and involves additional complexity
To validate consensus
4055.hint: Think about managing native tokens on the source chain.
4055.explanation: NativeTokenHome on the source L1 manages the native token locking process. When users send native tokens to the contract, it locks them as collateral and emits an ICM message. This message is relayed to the NativeTokenRemote on the destination L1, triggering native token minting there. The home contract tracks collateral for each registered remote and handles releasing native tokens when they are bridged back (when destination native tokens are burned).

alternates.question: How does NativeTokenHome track collateral across multiple registered remote contracts in a Native to Native bridge?
options: It uses a single global counter for all remotes
It maintains separate collateral balances for each registered remote, tracking how many native tokens are locked for each destination L1
It receives and locks native tokens, then coordinates via ICM messages for minting on the destination L1
It delegates collateral tracking to the P-Chain validators
alternates.hint: Think about what happens when one home contract serves multiple destination chains.
alternates.explanation: NativeTokenHome maintains per-remote accounting, tracking the amount of native tokens locked as collateral for each registered NativeTokenRemote contract. This is essential because a single home contract can serve multiple destination L1s. Each remote has its own collateral balance, registration status, and minting relationship. When tokens are bridged back from a specific remote, only that remote's collateral balance is reduced, ensuring accurate accounting across all bridge routes.

alternates.question: What happens when NativeTokenHome receives native tokens from a user during a bridge operation? (Select all that apply)
options: It wraps the native tokens into their ERC-20 equivalent (e.g., AVAX to WAVAX) for internal tracking
It locks the wrapped tokens as collateral in the contract
It coordinates ICM messages for native token minting on the destination
It immediately burns the native tokens to reduce the source chain supply
alternates.hint: Think about the two-step process of wrapping and locking that happens internally.
alternates.explanation: When NativeTokenHome receives native tokens via a payable function, it performs two internal steps: (1) wraps the native tokens into their ERC-20 equivalent (e.g., AVAX becomes WAVAX) because the contract needs ERC-20 interfaces for collateral management, and (2) locks these wrapped tokens as collateral within the contract. After locking, an ICM message is emitted for the relayer to pick up. The native tokens are not burned; they are preserved as locked collateral that can be released when users bridge back.

4056.question: How does NativeTokenRemote work in the Native to Native bridge?
options: It validates transactions on both chains
It only stores data
It receives ICM messages and uses the Native Minter precompile to mint native tokens on the destination L1 for users
It creates new blockchains, despite being a frequently cited but incorrect explanation and involves additional complexity
4056.hint: Think about creating native tokens on the destination.
4056.explanation: NativeTokenRemote receives ICM messages from the NativeTokenHome on the source L1. When it receives a bridge message, it uses the Native Minter precompile to mint native tokens and send them to the user's address on the destination L1. These minted tokens are actual native tokens that can be used to pay gas fees. When users bridge back, the remote contract burns the native tokens and sends a message to the home to unlock collateral. The contract must have Native Minter permissions configured during L1 setup.

alternates.question: What specific precompile function does NativeTokenRemote call to create native tokens on the destination L1?
options: It calls createNativeToken() on the chain's genesis contract
It receives ICM messages and uses the Native Minter precompile to mint native tokens on the destination L1
It calls transferNativeTokens() on the P-Chain
It calls mintNativeCoin(recipient, amount) on the Native Minter precompile at address 0x0200000000000000000000000000000000000001
alternates.hint: Think about the specific function signature and the precompile's fixed address.
alternates.explanation: NativeTokenRemote calls the mintNativeCoin(address, uint256) function on the Native Minter precompile at the fixed address 0x0200000000000000000000000000000000000001. This precompile is a built-in system contract on Avalanche L1s that operates at the protocol level, allowing authorized contracts to create new native tokens. The minted tokens appear in the recipient's balance as real native gas tokens, identical to tokens that existed at genesis.

alternates.question: How does the Native to Native bridge-back process work when a user wants to return native tokens from the destination L1? (Select all that apply)
options: The user transfers native tokens to a burn address, and NativeTokenHome automatically detects the burn
NativeTokenRemote burns the user's native tokens on the destination L1 and emits an ICM message
NativeTokenRemote uses the Native Minter precompile to mint native tokens when receiving bridge messages
NativeTokenHome receives the relayed ICM message and releases equivalent locked native tokens to the user on the source chain
alternates.hint: Think about the reverse flow: what happens on the destination and then on the source.
alternates.explanation: The bridge-back involves two key steps: (1) NativeTokenRemote burns the user's native tokens on the destination L1, reducing the supply, and emits an ICM message. (2) The relayer delivers this message to NativeTokenHome on the source chain, which releases the equivalent locked native tokens (unwrapping WAVAX back to AVAX, for example) and sends them to the user. This reverse process maintains the 1:1 backing invariant.

4057.question: In Native to Native bridging, why must you register the remote and add collateral before bridging?
options: This step is optional
To make the process slower, despite appearing correct based on incomplete information and involves additional complexity and involves additional complexity
To pay developers
Registration authorizes the bridge route and collateral ensures tokens are available to lock, maintaining the security and 1:1 backing model
4057.hint: Think about security and token backing.
4057.explanation: Registration is necessary to establish the authorized bridge route—the TokenHome needs to know which remotes are valid destinations to prevent tokens being sent to malicious addresses. Adding collateral ensures the home contract has native tokens available to lock when users bridge, maintaining the fundamental 1:1 backing between locked source tokens and minted destination tokens. Without collateral, there would be nothing to back the minted destination tokens, breaking the bridge's security model and potentially creating unbacked tokens.

alternates.question: What is the difference between registering a remote and adding collateral in the Native to Native bridge setup?
options: Collateral is deposited first, then registration happens automatically
They are the same operation performed in a single transaction
Registration authorizes the bridge route and collateral ensures 1:1 backing; these are the security and economic foundations of the model

Registration establishes the authorized bridge route by having NativeTokenHome recognize the remote, while collateral deposits the native tokens needed to back any pre-existing supply on the destination
alternates.hint: Think about the distinct purposes of each step in the setup process.
alternates.explanation: Registration and collateralization serve different purposes: Registration calls registerWithHome on the remote contract, which sends an ICM message to NativeTokenHome so it recognizes the remote as a valid bridge destination. This is a security measure that prevents unauthorized contracts from interacting with the home. Collateral is a separate step that deposits native tokens into the home contract to back any pre-existing native token supply (initialReserveImbalance) on the destination L1. Both must be completed before bridging is enabled.

alternates.question: What is the initialReserveImbalance parameter and when does it matter in the Native to Native bridge?
options: It specifies the maximum number of tokens that can be bridged per transaction
It represents the amount of pre-existing native tokens on the destination L1 that must be backed by collateral in NativeTokenHome before bridging is enabled
It is the gas fee multiplier for cross-chain transactions
Registration authorizes the bridge route and collateral ensures tokens are available, maintaining the security and 1:1 backing model
alternates.hint: Think about what happens if the destination L1 already has native tokens in circulation before the bridge is set up.
alternates.explanation: The initialReserveImbalance is set during NativeTokenRemote deployment and represents the amount of native tokens that already exist on the destination L1 (from the genesis allocation or prior minting). These tokens need to be backed by locked collateral on the source chain to maintain the 1:1 peg. The bridge will not enable minting until collateral equal to the initialReserveImbalance is deposited in NativeTokenHome. If set to 0, no initial collateral deposit is needed.

4058.question: What is the complete flow when bridging native tokens from L1-A to L1-B in a Native to Native bridge?
options: Tokens are copied to both chains
The chains merge temporarily
Tokens teleport instantly, although it might seem logical based on surface-level analysis and involves additional complexity and involves additional complexity
User sends native tokens to NativeTokenHome on L1-A (locked), relayer delivers ICM message to L1-B, and NativeTokenRemote mints native tokens for user on L1-B
4058.hint: Think about the complete lock, relay, mint sequence.
4058.explanation: The complete Native to Native bridge flow: 1) User sends native tokens to NativeTokenHome contract on L1-A, which locks them as collateral, 2) TokenHome emits an ICM message about the transfer, 3) Relayer observes L1-A, picks up the message, and submits it to L1-B, 4) NativeTokenRemote on L1-B receives the message and uses Native Minter precompile to mint equivalent native tokens to the user's address. The user now has native tokens on L1-B that can be used for gas. To bridge back, the process reverses—native tokens are burned on L1-B and unlocked on L1-A.

alternates.question: In the Native to Native bridge, what verification does NativeTokenHome perform when it receives an ICM message from a remote requesting token release? (Select all that apply)
options: It verifies the message came from a registered NativeTokenRemote contract address
User sends native tokens to NativeTokenHome on L1-A, relayer delivers ICM message, and NativeTokenRemote mints native tokens on L1-B
It verifies the message originated from a known blockchain ID that matches a registered remote
It does not verify anything; all ICM messages are trusted automatically
alternates.hint: Think about what prevents unauthorized contracts from tricking the home into releasing tokens.
alternates.explanation: When NativeTokenHome receives an ICM message requesting token release (for a bridge-back operation), it verifies that the message originated from a registered remote contract on a recognized blockchain ID. This verification prevents malicious contracts from sending fake messages to steal locked tokens. The registration step creates this trust relationship by recording the remote's contract address and blockchain ID, which are checked against every incoming message.

alternates.question: How many ICM messages are involved in a complete round-trip (bridge out and bridge back) in the Native to Native bridge?
options: One message handles both directions
User sends native tokens to NativeTokenHome which locks them, relayer delivers ICM message, and NativeTokenRemote mints native tokens
At least three: one for registration, one for the bridge-out (lock and mint), and one for the bridge-back (burn and release)
Two: one from NativeTokenHome to NativeTokenRemote for bridge-out, and one from NativeTokenRemote to NativeTokenHome for bridge-back
alternates.hint: Think about each direction of the bridge requiring its own cross-chain message.
alternates.explanation: A complete round-trip involves two ICM messages (not counting the initial registration): (1) Bridge-out: NativeTokenHome locks tokens and emits an ICM message delivered to NativeTokenRemote, which mints native tokens. (2) Bridge-back: NativeTokenRemote burns native tokens and emits an ICM message delivered to NativeTokenHome, which releases the locked tokens. Registration is a separate one-time setup message. Each ICM message requires a relayer to physically deliver it across chains.

4059.question: What is the key concept of permissioned L1s that distinguishes them from permissionless L1s? (Select all that apply)
options: There is no difference, which would actually introduce several security vulnerabilities and involves additional complexity and involves additional complexity
Permissioned L1s use Proof of Authority where a specific set of validators are authorized to validate, rather than open participation through staking
Permissioned L1s are faster
Permissioned L1s are always on testnet
4059.hint: Think about who can become a validator.
4059.explanation: Permissioned L1s use a Proof of Authority model where only specific authorized validators can validate the blockchain. The L1 owner explicitly adds or removes validators using the Validator Manager contract. This differs from permissionless L1s which use Proof of Stake, allowing anyone to become a validator by staking tokens. Permissioned L1s are suitable for consortiums, enterprise use cases, or applications requiring known validators.

alternates.question: What is the two-phase commit pattern used by the Validator Manager Contract in permissioned L1s?
options: Deploy the contract, then initialize it with parameters
Permissioned L1s use Proof of Authority where a specific set of validators are authorized rather than open participation
Every validator change requires an initiate step (sending a request to P-Chain) and a complete step (finalizing after P-Chain confirmation), ensuring cross-chain consistency
Validators vote on the change, then the owner approves it
alternates.hint: Think about how validator changes are synchronized between the Validator Manager Contract and the P-Chain.
alternates.explanation: The Validator Manager Contract uses a two-phase commit pattern for all validator changes: First, an initiate function (e.g., initiateValidatorRegistration) sends a Warp message to the P-Chain requesting the change. Second, after the P-Chain processes and confirms the request, a complete function (e.g., completeValidatorRegistration) is called to finalize the state update on the contract. This pattern ensures the contract state and P-Chain state remain synchronized and prevents inconsistencies.

alternates.question: In a permissioned L1 using Proof of Authority, who can call the validator management functions on the Validator Manager Contract? (Select all that apply)
options: Anyone who stakes enough tokens
Only the owner address (EOA or multi-sig) protected by the onlyOwner modifier
Permissioned L1s use Proof of Authority where specific validators are authorized to validate
The owner can add validators, remove validators, and update validator weights through the contract
alternates.hint: Think about the access control model in Proof of Authority.
alternates.explanation: In a PoA-based permissioned L1, all validator management functions (initiateValidatorRegistration, initiateValidatorRemoval, initiateValidatorWeightUpdate) are protected by the onlyOwner modifier. This means only the designated owner address, which can be an EOA or a multi-sig, can call these functions. The owner has full control to add validators, remove them, and change their voting weights. This centralized control is the defining characteristic of PoA and the key difference from PoS, where these functions are open to anyone who stakes tokens.

4060.question: What are the key L1 native tokenomics concepts needed to understand before building a permissionless L1?
options: Tokenomics don't matter for permissionless L1s
Only staking matters, but this interpretation oversimplifies the actual mechanism and involves additional complexity and involves additional complexity
Only gas fees matter
Understanding native tokens vs ERC-20, custom gas tokens, wrapped tokens, precompiles like Native Minter, and token utility are all important
4060.hint: Think about all the token-related features an L1 needs.
4060.explanation: Before building a permissionless L1, you need to understand: 1) The difference between native tokens (protocol-level) and ERC-20 tokens (contract-level), 2) How custom gas tokens work and can be configured, 3) Wrapped tokens for DeFi compatibility, 4) Precompiles like Native Minter that enable custom tokenomics, and 5) Token utility (gas, staking, governance). These concepts are foundational because permissionless L1s typically have their own tokens for staking rewards and network incentives.

alternates.question: Why might an L1 choose to use a different token for staking versus gas fees?
options: Understanding native tokens, custom gas tokens, wrapped tokens, precompiles, and token utility are all important for building a permissionless L1
To bootstrap security using an established liquid token like AVAX, especially when the native gas token may not have sufficient liquidity for economic security
There is no valid reason to separate staking and gas tokens
To ensure validators cannot pay for transactions on the L1
alternates.hint: Think about the liquidity and economic security requirements for a staking token.
alternates.explanation: An L1 might use a different staking token when its native gas token lacks sufficient liquidity for economic security. A low-liquidity staking token creates a vulnerability where an attacker could cheaply acquire a majority stake and take over the network. Using an established token like AVAX provides pre-existing liquidity and market depth, making attacks prohibitively expensive. However, this separation means the gas token and staking token serve different economic functions, requiring careful design of both systems.

alternates.question: What is the liquidity attack vector that L1 builders must consider when selecting a staking token?
options: Attackers can exploit low gas fees to spam the network with transactions
All token-related features like native tokens, custom gas tokens, and precompiles must be understood for permissionless L1s
Attackers can manipulate the Native Minter precompile to mint unlimited tokens
Attackers can acquire a majority of a low-liquidity staking token cheaply, gaining control of consensus to halt the network, double-spend, or censor transactions
alternates.hint: Think about what happens when it is cheap to acquire most of the staking token supply.
alternates.explanation: The liquidity attack vector occurs when an L1 uses a low-liquidity token for staking. An attacker could take a large loan or use existing capital to purchase a majority (51%+) of the staking token at low cost due to thin markets. With majority stake, they control consensus and can halt the network, double-spend, censor transactions, or extract value. They might even short the native token before attacking, profiting from the resulting price crash. This is why L1 builders must evaluate market capitalization, daily trading volume, market depth, and token distribution when choosing a staking token.

4061.question: What are the main deployment options for Validator Manager Contracts?
options: Validator Manager Contracts cannot be deployed
Only deployment on testnet is available, which contradicts the fundamental design principles involved and involves additional complexity
Deployment on C-Chain (for mainnet L1s) or Fuji C-Chain (for testnet L1s), with the contract address used to convert a subnet to an L1
Deployment on any blockchain
4061.hint: Think about where the P-Chain reads validator information from.
4061.explanation: Validator Manager Contracts are deployed on the C-Chain (for mainnet L1s) or Fuji C-Chain (for testnet L1s). After deployment, the contract address is used when converting a subnet to an L1 on the P-Chain. This links the L1 to the Validator Manager Contract, which then controls the validator set. The P-Chain reads validator information from this contract, enabling programmatic validator management through smart contracts instead of P-Chain transactions.

alternates.question: Why are Validator Manager Contracts deployed on C-Chain rather than on the L1 itself?
options: Because the P-Chain reads validator information from contracts on the C-Chain, and the contract address is used when converting a subnet to an L1 on the P-Chain
Deployment on C-Chain or Fuji C-Chain is required, with the contract address used to convert a subnet to an L1
C-Chain has lower gas fees than any L1
The L1 doesn't exist yet at the time of initial deployment, since the Validator Manager must be in place before the L1 can be created
alternates.hint: Think about which chain the P-Chain interacts with for validator information.
alternates.explanation: Validator Manager Contracts are deployed on the C-Chain because the P-Chain reads validator information from this contract. When converting a subnet to an L1, the contract address on C-Chain is provided to the P-Chain, establishing the link between the L1 and its validator management. The P-Chain then monitors this contract for validator set changes. While the L1 may not exist yet at the time of initial deployment, the fundamental reason is the P-Chain's integration with C-Chain for reading validator state.

alternates.question: What happens after a Validator Manager Contract is deployed on C-Chain and linked to an L1?
options: The contract automatically begins accepting staking deposits
The L1 validators take over management of the contract
Deployment on C-Chain or Fuji C-Chain provides the contract address used to convert a subnet to an L1
The P-Chain monitors the contract for validator set changes, reading validator registrations, removals, and weight updates submitted through the contract
alternates.hint: Think about the ongoing relationship between the P-Chain and the Validator Manager Contract.
alternates.explanation: Once the Validator Manager Contract is deployed on C-Chain and linked to the L1 via the ConvertSubnetToL1 P-Chain transaction, the P-Chain continuously monitors it for validator set changes. When the contract initiates a validator registration, removal, or weight update, the P-Chain processes these requests through Warp messages. This programmatic validator management replaces the need for direct P-Chain transactions, enabling features like PoA and PoS through smart contract logic.

4062.question: What is Proof of Stake and how does it differ from Proof of Authority?
options: Proof of Stake allows anyone to become a validator by staking tokens, while Proof of Authority uses a permissioned set of known validators
They are the same consensus mechanism, which is a common misconception in distributed systems and involves additional complexity and involves additional complexity
Proof of Stake requires no tokens
Proof of Authority is always better
4062.hint: Think about permissionless participation vs. authorized validators.
4062.explanation: Proof of Stake (PoS) is a consensus mechanism where anyone can become a validator by staking the required amount of tokens, making it permissionless. Validators are economically incentivized to behave honestly because they risk losing their stake. Proof of Authority (PoA), used by permissioned L1s, relies on a specific set of authorized validators chosen by the chain operator. PoS enables decentralization and open participation, while PoA offers control and known validators but is more centralized.

alternates.question: On the Avalanche Primary Network, what is the minimum stake required to become a validator and what uptime is needed for rewards?
options: No minimum stake is required; uptime does not affect rewards
Proof of Stake allows anyone to become a validator by staking tokens, while Proof of Authority uses a permissioned set of known validators
100 AVAX minimum stake with 99% uptime requirement
2,000 AVAX minimum validator stake on Mainnet with a default 80% uptime requirement to receive staking rewards
alternates.hint: Think about the specific staking parameters mentioned for the Avalanche Mainnet.
alternates.explanation: On the Avalanche Mainnet, validators must stake a minimum of 2,000 AVAX to participate in consensus. To receive staking rewards, validators need to maintain at least 80% uptime (the default requirement). The staking duration can be between 2 weeks and 1 year. These parameters create economic incentives for validators to maintain reliable infrastructure while keeping the barrier to entry accessible enough for broad participation.

alternates.question: How does Avalanche's PoS differ from traditional Delegated Proof of Stake (DPoS) systems?
options: In Avalanche PoS, anyone meeting the minimum stake can become a validator without an election; DPoS systems elect a fixed number of delegates through a voting mechanism
PoS allows anyone to become a validator by staking tokens while PoA uses a permissioned set of known validators
Avalanche PoS is identical to DPoS in its validator selection mechanism
Avalanche PoS does not support delegation, while DPoS does
alternates.hint: Think about how validators are selected in each system.
alternates.explanation: Avalanche PoS is more permissionless than DPoS. In DPoS systems, token holders vote to elect a fixed number of delegates who validate blocks. In Avalanche, there is no voting or election; anyone who stakes the minimum amount (2,000 AVAX on Mainnet) automatically becomes a validator. Avalanche does support delegation (minimum 25 AVAX, with validators charging at least 2% fees), but delegation increases a validator's weight rather than electing them. This makes Avalanche more decentralized since there is no cap on the number of validators.

4063.question: What role does the staking token play in a Proof of Stake L1? (Select all that apply)
options: It's only used for paying gas fees
Only validators receive staking tokens
It has no role, though this reasoning fails to account for key constraints and involves additional complexity and involves additional complexity
The staking token must be locked by validators as collateral to participate in consensus, aligning economic incentives and securing the network
4063.hint: Think about validator collateral and economic alignment.
4063.explanation: In Proof of Stake, the staking token serves as economic collateral that validators must lock to participate in consensus. This stake can be slashed (partially or fully confiscated) if validators behave maliciously, creating a strong economic incentive for honest behavior. The staking token aligns validator incentives with network security—the more valuable the token, the more valuable their stake and the greater the cost of misbehavior. The staking token can be the same as the gas token or a different token, depending on the L1's design.

alternates.question: Can the staking token be different from the native gas token on an Avalanche L1, and what are the implications?
options: No, the staking token must always be the native gas token
The staking token must be locked as collateral to participate in consensus, aligning economic incentives and securing the network
Yes, Avalanche L1s support using a separate ERC-20 token for staking while having a different native gas token, but the staking token must have sufficient liquidity to prevent economic attacks
Yes, but only if the gas token is AVAX
alternates.hint: Think about the flexibility Avalanche L1s offer and the security considerations.
alternates.explanation: Avalanche L1s offer the flexibility to use either the native gas token or a separate ERC-20 token for staking. If using a separate token, it must have substantial liquidity to prevent the liquidity attack vector where an attacker cheaply acquires majority stake. The ERC20TokenStakingManager contract supports this pattern. Some L1s may choose to use AVAX or another established token for staking to bootstrap security, while maintaining a separate native token optimized for gas payments.

alternates.question: What determines a validator's influence during the consensus polling process on Avalanche?
options: The validator's hardware specifications and network bandwidth
The validator's weight, which is the sum of their own staked tokens plus all tokens delegated to them by others
The staking token must be locked by validators as collateral to participate in consensus and secure the network
The order in which the validator registered on the network
alternates.hint: Think about how staking amount and delegation combine to determine influence.
alternates.explanation: A validator's influence in Avalanche consensus is determined by their validator weight, which is calculated as the sum of their own staked tokens plus all tokens delegated to them. During consensus, validators are polled in proportion to their weight. A validator with 10,000 AVAX (own stake + delegations) has more influence than one with 3,000 AVAX. This stake-weighted polling ensures that those with the most economic skin in the game have the greatest influence on consensus outcomes.

4064.question: What is liquid staking and why is it important for PoS L1s?
options: Liquid staking is the same as regular staking, which is an oversimplification of the actual process and involves additional complexity and involves additional complexity
Liquid staking refers to staking in water
Liquid staking allows users to stake tokens while receiving a liquid representative token they can use in DeFi, solving the capital efficiency problem
Liquid staking eliminates the need for validators
4064.hint: Think about the opportunity cost of locked staking tokens.
4064.explanation: Liquid staking solves the capital efficiency problem where staked tokens are locked and cannot be used elsewhere. Users deposit tokens into a liquid staking protocol and receive a representative token (like stAVAX for AVAX) that can be used in DeFi, traded, or used as collateral while the underlying tokens remain staked. This increases participation in staking by reducing the opportunity cost, improves capital efficiency in the ecosystem, and enhances network security by making staking more attractive.

alternates.question: What is the consensus risk associated with liquid staking, particularly on smaller chains?
options: Liquid staking tokens cannot be used in DeFi applications
There are no risks associated with liquid staking
Liquid staking allows users to stake while receiving a liquid representative token for use in DeFi, solving capital efficiency
Recursive leverage, where liquid staking tokens are used as collateral to repeatedly borrow and stake, can lead to outsized influence over network consensus
alternates.hint: Think about what happens when someone uses their liquid staking token as collateral to stake again.
alternates.explanation: Recursive leverage is a significant consensus risk with liquid staking. A user can stake tokens, receive a liquid staking token (e.g., stAVAX), use that token as collateral to borrow more tokens, stake those, and repeat. This cycle can give a single actor outsized influence over consensus, especially dangerous on smaller chains. Mitigations include limiting loan-to-value ratios, applying rate limits on staking, and promoting validator decentralization to prevent concentration of staking power.

alternates.question: Which of the following are liquid staking providers in the Avalanche ecosystem? (Select all that apply)
options: Gogopool, which offers liquid staking with security-audited smart contracts
Liquid staking solves capital efficiency by letting users receive representative tokens while their assets remain staked
Uniswap, which handles token swaps on Avalanche
Liquid staking providers issue derivative tokens like stAVAX that represent the staked assets
alternates.hint: Think about the specific providers mentioned and what liquid staking tokens represent.
alternates.explanation: Gogopool is a prominent liquid staking provider in the Avalanche ecosystem, offering secure smart contracts with competitive staking yields. Liquid staking providers issue derivative tokens (like stAVAX for staked AVAX) that represent the underlying staked assets. These derivative tokens can be traded, used as collateral, or deployed in DeFi while the original tokens continue earning staking rewards. Uniswap is a DEX, not a liquid staking provider.

4065.question: What are the main requirements to transform a permissioned L1 into a permissionless PoS L1?
options: No requirements, it happens automatically, which contradicts the fundamental design principles involved and involves additional complexity and involves additional complexity
Enable Native Minter precompile for minting staking rewards, deploy a Reward Manager, and deploy a Staking Manager (PoS Validator Manager) contract
Just deploy new contracts
Replace all validators immediately
4065.hint: Think about what infrastructure is needed for staking and rewards.
4065.explanation: Transforming to a permissionless PoS L1 requires: 1) Native Minter precompile must be enabled so contracts can mint tokens as staking rewards, 2) A Reward Manager contract to calculate and distribute rewards, and 3) A PoS Validator Manager (Staking Manager) contract to handle stake deposits, validator registration, and coordinate with the P-Chain. These components create the infrastructure for permissionless participation, staking mechanics, and economic incentives that secure a PoS network.

alternates.question: What happens to transaction fees on a PoS L1 if the Reward Manager precompile is not enabled?
options: Transaction fees are sent to the contract deployer
Transaction fees are distributed equally among all validators
Enable Native Minter precompile for minting staking rewards, deploy a Reward Manager, and deploy a PoS Validator Manager contract
Transaction fees are simply burned rather than distributed as rewards, eliminating a potential economic incentive for validators
alternates.hint: Think about what the course says about the Reward Manager being optional but recommended.
alternates.explanation: If the Reward Manager precompile is not enabled, transaction fees are burned instead of being distributed as rewards. While the Reward Manager is optional, without it validators receive no staking rewards and only get their original stake back when unstaking. This significantly weakens the PoS security model because there is no economic incentive beyond basic operation, reducing decentralization as fewer participants are motivated to stake and validate.

alternates.question: In the PoA to PoS transformation, what changes about the validator registration functions?
options: The functions are removed entirely and replaced with new ones
The Staking Manager opens validator management functions to the public but requires economic stake instead of owner permission, enabling anyone to register as a validator by staking tokens
The functions remain restricted to the owner but add a staking requirement
Enable Native Minter precompile, deploy a Reward Manager, and deploy a Staking Manager to transform permissioned to permissionless
alternates.hint: Think about how access control changes from owner-only to public with economic requirements.
alternates.explanation: In PoA, functions like initiateValidatorRegistration are protected by the onlyOwner modifier, meaning only the designated owner can add validators. When transforming to PoS, the Staking Manager contract opens these functions to the public but replaces the permission requirement with an economic requirement: callers must stake the minimum required tokens. Ownership of the Validator Manager is transferred to the Staking Manager, which then manages all validator operations based on staking logic rather than owner authorization.

4066.question: Why is the Native Minter precompile essential for PoS L1s with staking rewards?
options: It's not essential
It burns tokens
It allows authorized contracts like the Staking Manager to mint new native tokens as rewards for validators and delegators
It validates blocks, but this does not accurately reflect the underlying architecture and involves additional complexity and involves additional complexity
4066.hint: Think about where staking rewards come from.
4066.explanation: The Native Minter precompile is essential because it enables smart contracts to mint new native tokens to pay staking rewards. When validators and delegators stake tokens to secure the network, they expect rewards. The Staking Manager contract uses the Native Minter precompile to create these rewards by minting new tokens. Without this precompile, there would be no mechanism to generate staking rewards at the protocol level, making it impossible to incentivize validators in a PoS model.

alternates.question: What is the specific function call chain when the NativeTokenStakingManager distributes staking rewards?
options: The NativeTokenStakingManager's internal _reward() function calls nativeMinter.mintNativeCoin(recipient, amount) on the precompile at 0x0200000000000000000000000000000000000001
It calls transfer() on a pre-funded reward pool contract
It calls authorized contracts like the Staking Manager to mint new native tokens as rewards for validators
It calls the P-Chain to release staked tokens plus rewards
alternates.hint: Think about the _reward() function implementation and which precompile it calls.
alternates.explanation: When distributing staking rewards, the NativeTokenStakingManager uses its internal _reward(address account, uint256 amount) function, which calls nativeMinter.mintNativeCoin(account, amount) on the Native Minter precompile at address 0x0200000000000000000000000000000000000001. This creates new native tokens at the protocol level and sends them directly to the recipient's address. The contract must have admin privileges on the precompile for this call to succeed.

alternates.question: What is the difference between how NativeTokenStakingManager and ERC20TokenStakingManager handle reward distribution?
options: NativeTokenStakingManager distributes from a pre-funded pool, while ERC20TokenStakingManager mints new tokens
NativeTokenStakingManager mints new native tokens via the Native Minter precompile, while ERC20TokenStakingManager mints new ERC-20 tokens through the staking token's mint function
Both use the same mechanism; there is no difference
The Native Minter precompile allows authorized contracts to mint new native tokens as staking rewards
alternates.hint: Think about the different token types each manager handles and how new tokens are created.
alternates.explanation: The two staking managers distribute rewards differently based on their token type: NativeTokenStakingManager calls the Native Minter precompile to mint new native tokens (protocol-level operation), while ERC20TokenStakingManager calls the staking token's mint function (since the ERC-20 token must implement IERC20Mintable). Both create new tokens rather than distributing from a pool, but they use different mechanisms because native tokens and ERC-20 tokens are minted differently in the EVM.

4067.question: What is the role of the Reward Manager in a PoS L1? (Select all that apply)
options: To deploy smart contracts
To calculate and manage the distribution of staking rewards to validators and delegators based on their stake and performance
To validate transactions, but this fails to consider the consensus requirements involved and involves additional complexity and involves additional complexity
To store user data
4067.hint: Think about the logic for distributing rewards fairly.
4067.explanation: The Reward Manager calculates and manages the distribution of staking rewards to validators and delegators. It implements the reward calculation logic (such as proportional to stake, uptime, etc.), tracks who should receive rewards, and coordinates with the Staking Manager to actually distribute them. The Reward Manager might be a separate contract or integrated into the Staking Manager, depending on the implementation. It ensures rewards are distributed fairly based on stake amount, duration, and validator performance.

alternates.question: What factors does the Reward Calculator consider when determining staking rewards?
options: To calculate and manage the distribution of staking rewards based on stake and performance
Staking duration, stake amount (validator weight), current total token supply, and configured reward rate parameters
Only the number of transactions validated during the staking period
Only the validator's uptime percentage
alternates.hint: Think about all the variables that go into the reward formula.
alternates.explanation: The Reward Calculator determines rewards based on multiple factors: (1) Staking duration, how long the tokens were staked, (2) Stake amount, the validator's weight including delegated tokens, (3) Current supply, as the reward rate typically decreases with increasing supply to control inflation, and (4) Network parameters, the configured reward rates and caps. The general formula is: reward = (stake_amount * staking_duration * reward_rate) / supply_factor.

alternates.question: What happens during the abort path of the reward distribution process on the P-Chain?
options: The Reward Manager calculates and distributes staking rewards to validators and delegators based on their performance
All staked tokens are permanently burned as a penalty
The validator receives only their original stake without validation rewards, and the potential reward amount is burned (decreasing supply), though accumulated delegation fees are still returned
The abort path does not exist; all validators always receive rewards
alternates.hint: Think about what happens during network instability and how the system protects itself.
alternates.explanation: The abort path protects the network during unstable periods. When a RewardValidatorTx takes the abort path: the validator receives only their original staked tokens (no validation rewards), the potential reward amount is burned by decreasing the supply, and any accumulated delegatee rewards from past delegators are still returned. This mechanism prevents rewarding validators during periods of network issues, maintaining economic integrity while not permanently penalizing delegators who had already completed their staking periods.

4068.question: What is the purpose of the 'create L1 speedrun' exercise in the permissionless L1s course?
options: To win a competition
To quickly practice creating an L1 from scratch, reinforcing the core concepts of L1 creation before adding PoS complexity
To deploy on mainnet, although some early documentation may have suggested otherwise and involves additional complexity and involves additional complexity
It's not an important exercise
4068.hint: Think about learning by doing before adding complexity.
4068.explanation: The create L1 speedrun provides hands-on practice to quickly create a basic L1 from scratch, reinforcing fundamental concepts like using Avalanche CLI, configuring VMs, and deploying to testnet. By mastering basic L1 creation in a streamlined exercise, students build confidence before tackling the additional complexity of adding PoS, staking managers, and reward systems. It's a pedagogical stepping stone that ensures solid understanding of the foundation before layering on advanced features.

alternates.question: What specific skills does the 'create L1 speedrun' exercise reinforce before students tackle PoS?
options: Smart contract development and Solidity programming
Using Avalanche CLI to configure VMs, set genesis parameters, and deploy an L1 to testnet, establishing the foundation for later PoS complexity
Quickly practice creating an L1 from scratch to reinforce core concepts before adding PoS complexity
Setting up validator nodes and configuring consensus parameters
alternates.hint: Think about the practical tools and configuration steps involved in basic L1 creation.
alternates.explanation: The create L1 speedrun reinforces practical skills with the Avalanche CLI: configuring the VM (choosing EVM parameters), setting genesis parameters (initial token supply, precompiles), and deploying the L1 to the Fuji testnet. These foundational skills are essential because the PoS transformation builds on top of a working L1. Students need to be comfortable with the basic deployment workflow before adding the complexity of staking managers, reward calculators, and Native Minter permissions.

alternates.question: Why is the speedrun exercise structured as a fast-paced practice rather than a detailed tutorial?
options: To practice creating an L1 from scratch, reinforcing core concepts before adding PoS complexity
Because detailed tutorials are provided elsewhere in the course
To verify that students can independently perform L1 creation steps they learned previously, confirming readiness for the advanced PoS material
Because L1 creation is a trivial task that doesn't need detailed explanation
alternates.hint: Think about the pedagogical purpose of a speedrun versus a step-by-step tutorial.
alternates.explanation: The speedrun format serves as a readiness check: it verifies students can independently perform L1 creation without hand-holding, confirming they have internalized the knowledge from earlier courses. If a student struggles with the speedrun, it signals they need to review fundamentals before proceeding to PoS. The fast pace also builds confidence and muscle memory with the CLI tools, which students will use extensively in the more complex staking manager setup that follows.

4069.question: What is the focus of the 'permissioned L1 speedrun' exercise?
options: Writing smart contracts
Quickly deploying a permissioned L1 with Proof of Authority using the PoA Validator Manager, practicing the baseline before adding PoS
Creating ERC-20 tokens, which would actually introduce several security vulnerabilities and involves additional complexity and involves additional complexity
Running mainnet validators
4069.hint: Think about mastering PoA before PoS.
4069.explanation: The permissioned L1 speedrun focuses on quickly deploying a Proof of Authority L1 using the PoA Validator Manager contract. This exercise ensures students understand the baseline validator management model before transitioning to the more complex PoS model. It covers deploying the Validator Manager, adding authorized validators, and managing the validator set—skills that provide context for understanding how PoS validator management differs and builds upon PoA concepts.

alternates.question: What is the PoA Validator Manager contract and what functions does it protect with the onlyOwner modifier?
options: It manages gas fee configuration and protects fee-related functions
It controls initiateValidatorRegistration, initiateValidatorRemoval, and initiateValidatorWeightUpdate, restricting these to the owner address
Quickly deploying a permissioned L1 with PoA using the Validator Manager, practicing the baseline before adding PoS
It manages ERC-20 token transfers and protects mint/burn functions
alternates.hint: Think about which validator lifecycle functions are restricted in a PoA model.
alternates.explanation: The PoA Validator Manager contract restricts critical validator management functions behind the onlyOwner modifier. The owner (an EOA or multi-sig) has exclusive control over: initiateValidatorRegistration (adding new validators), initiateValidatorRemoval (removing validators), and initiateValidatorWeightUpdate (changing a validator's voting weight). Understanding these restricted functions is key to understanding how PoS transforms them from owner-only to publicly accessible with staking requirements.

alternates.question: Why does the permissioned L1 speedrun come before the PoS transformation in the course sequence? (Select all that apply)
options: Deploying a permissioned L1 with PoA using the Validator Manager provides the baseline before adding PoS
Students need a working permissioned L1 as the starting point that will be transformed into a permissionless PoS L1
It ensures students understand the PoA Validator Manager's onlyOwner access model so they can appreciate what changes when transitioning to PoS
Permissioned L1s are always deployed before permissionless ones in production
alternates.hint: Think about what the PoS transformation requires as input and what knowledge it builds upon.
alternates.explanation: The permissioned L1 speedrun serves two purposes: (1) It produces a working permissioned L1 that becomes the actual starting point for the PoS transformation exercise that follows, so students need a live chain to work with. (2) It ensures students understand the PoA access model, specifically how validator functions are restricted to the owner, so they can clearly see the contrast when PoS opens these same functions to anyone with sufficient stake.

4070.question: What components must be deployed during Staking Manager setup for a PoS L1?
options: No contracts are needed, but this fails to consider the consensus requirements involved and involves additional complexity and involves additional complexity
A Staking Manager contract, a Reward Calculator contract, and configuration of the Native Minter precompile with proper permissions
Only a single staking contract
Just update the existing Validator Manager
4070.hint: Think about all the pieces needed for staking and rewards.
4070.explanation: Setting up a PoS L1 requires deploying: 1) The Staking Manager contract (PoS Validator Manager) that handles stake deposits, validator registration, and P-Chain coordination, 2) A Reward Calculator contract that implements the reward distribution logic, and 3) Proper configuration of the Native Minter precompile to authorize the Staking Manager to mint reward tokens. These components work together to create a complete PoS system with staking, validation, and rewards.

alternates.question: What is the ExampleRewardCalculator contract and what role does it play in the Staking Manager setup?
options: It is the main staking contract that handles validator registration
A Staking Manager, Reward Calculator, and Native Minter configuration must all be deployed for PoS setup
It is a contract that determines staking reward amounts based on validator uptime, and its address is provided to the Staking Manager during initialization
It is an optional monitoring tool for tracking validator performance
alternates.hint: Think about what determines how much reward each validator receives.
alternates.explanation: The ExampleRewardCalculator is a dedicated contract that implements the reward calculation logic, determining how staking rewards are distributed based on validator uptime. During Staking Manager initialization, the ExampleRewardCalculator's address is passed as a parameter so the Staking Manager knows where to query for reward amounts. This separation allows the reward logic to be customized or upgraded independently of the core staking infrastructure.

alternates.question: In what order must the Staking Manager setup steps be performed, and why does order matter?
options: The order does not matter; all components can be deployed in any sequence

Deploy Staking Manager, deploy Reward Calculator, initialize Staking Manager with configuration, enable Native Minter permissions, then transfer ownership, because each step depends on outputs from previous steps
A Staking Manager contract, Reward Calculator contract, and Native Minter configuration with proper permissions must be deployed
Initialize first, then deploy contracts, then enable permissions
alternates.hint: Think about which addresses and configurations are needed at each step.
alternates.explanation: The order matters because of dependencies: (1) Deploy Staking Manager first to get its contract address. (2) Deploy Reward Calculator to get its address. (3) Initialize the Staking Manager with the Reward Calculator address and staking parameters. (4) Enable the Staking Manager's address on the Native Minter precompile so it can mint rewards. (5) Transfer Validator Manager ownership to the Staking Manager so it can manage validators. Each step requires the output (addresses or configuration) from preceding steps.

4071.question: What does the Staking Manager contract control in a PoS L1? (Select all that apply)
options: Validator registration, stake deposits/withdrawals, delegation, coordination with P-Chain for validator set updates, and reward distribution
Just block validation
Only gas fees
Nothing important, although some early documentation may have suggested otherwise and involves additional complexity and involves additional complexity
4071.hint: Think about all validator and staking lifecycle operations.
4071.explanation: The Staking Manager contract is the core of PoS L1 operations, controlling: 1) Validator registration and onboarding, 2) Stake deposits and withdrawals for both validators and delegators, 3) Delegation allowing users to stake with validators, 4) Coordination with the P-Chain to update the validator set, and 5) Reward calculation and distribution using the Reward Calculator and Native Minter. It's the central smart contract that enables permissionless participation and manages the entire staking lifecycle.

alternates.question: How does the Staking Manager coordinate with the P-Chain for validator set updates?
options: The P-Chain automatically detects staking changes without any communication
The Staking Manager sends Warp messages to the P-Chain when validators register or are removed, and receives P-Chain confirmations through a two-phase commit pattern
Validator registration, stake deposits, delegation, P-Chain coordination, and reward distribution are all controlled by the Staking Manager
Validators must manually update their registration on the P-Chain
alternates.hint: Think about how smart contract state changes are communicated to the P-Chain.
alternates.explanation: The Staking Manager coordinates with the P-Chain through Warp messages in a two-phase commit pattern: When a validator registers by staking tokens, the Staking Manager initiates the process and sends a Warp message to the P-Chain. After the P-Chain processes and confirms the registration, the complete function is called to finalize the validator addition. The same pattern applies for validator removal and weight updates. This ensures consistency between the contract's validator set and the P-Chain's authoritative record.

alternates.question: What is the difference between validator staking and delegation in the Staking Manager? (Select all that apply)
options: Validators call initiateValidatorRegistration with their stake to run a node, while delegators call initiateDelegatorRegistration to add stake to an existing validator without running infrastructure
Delegation increases a validator's total weight, giving them more influence in consensus
Validator registration, stake management, delegation, and reward distribution are all managed by the Staking Manager
There is no difference; both validators and delegators run validator nodes
alternates.hint: Think about who runs infrastructure and how delegation affects validator weight.
